## Page 213

PART I
EXPLANATORY NOTES AND DEFINITIONS OF
TERMS USED IN THE REPORT
AREA
The acreages given are as measured by the Ordnance Survey and relate to the
County Borough and its wards as constituted at the date of the 1961 Census.
Fore-
shore is excluded.
Acreages of areas covered by tidal water are also excluded from
the body of Table 4, but the total is given in a footnote thereto.
POPULATION
The 1961 Census population comprises all living persons, irrespective of their
usual address or nationality, who spent Census night in the area of enumeration.
Members of the Armed Forces (including those of Commonwealth and foreign countries
except personnel aboard foreign naval vessels) are included.
Persons travelling
on Census night were enumerated with the population of the district in which they
arrived the next day unless they had already been enumerated elsewhere.
Persons
on vessels at moorings or anchorage in Northern Ireland waters or aboard ships on
coastwise voyage to Northern Ireland ports were counted as part of the population
of the district containing the mooring, anchorage or arrival port.
Population figures for the 1951 Census are adjusted to relate to areas as con-
stituted at the date of the 1961 Census.
Figures for earlier Censuses relate,
generally, to areas as constituted at the respective Census dates.
ADMINISTRATIVE DIVISIONS
The County Borough is divided into fifteen wards which form the units for
borough council elections.
It is also divided into sixteen parliamentary
constituencies for elections to the Northern Ireland Parliament and into four
constituencies for elections to the United Kingdom Parliament.
The relationship
between the wards and the parliamentary constituencies is shown in a footnote to
Table 11.
No changes affecting Census statistics were made in the boundaries of the
County Borough or its wards during the last intercensal period.
BUILDINGS FDR HABITATION
For enumeration record purposes, a building was broadly defined as a structure
comprising one or more rooms or other spaces enclosed within external or party walls.
A structure detached from but subsidiary to a main building was regarded as part of
the main building unless it contained residential accommodation normally occupied by
a separate household.
Non-permanent structures such as huts, shacks and converted
railway carriages and mobile structures such as tents and caravans were treated as
buildings only if they were occupied on Census night or were some person's usual
residence.
Unoccupied structures of wood, asbestos, corrugated iron, etc., used as
casual residences, e.g. at week-ends or holiday times, were regarded as buildings
if they appeared to be of sound construction and had amenities such as piped water,
sewerage facilities and electric lighting.
Dilapidated structures which were unoccupied and unlikely to be used again were
ignored.
Buildings in course of erection were listed only if some part thereof was
in use as residential accommodation.
(73488)
Vil


---

## Page 214

Buildings for habitation comprise all buildings used wholly or partly as
residential accommodation.
They are divided into two categories:-
(i) Private dwellings consisting of structurally separate units of living
accommodation occupied or normally occupied by private households.
They
include houses, flats, non-permanent and mobile structures, and accommodation
in buildings which are not wholly residential.
Married quarters in Service
establishments are also included.
A building is regarded as not wholly residential if part of it is used
exclusively or primarily for purposes other than living accommodation, for
example, a factory or block of offices containing a director's or caretaker's
flat, or a building comprising a shop and dwelling.
Buildings such as a
doctor's or dentist's house with a room or annexe used as a surgery and a
house containing a clergyman's or author's study are, however, treated as
wholly residential.
(ii) Other buildings such as hotels, hostels, boarding houses, hospitals,
welfare centres, religious community premises, boarding schools and other
premises which exist for a specific functional purpose in addition to pro-
viding residential accommodation.
Civilian shipping and establishments under
Naval, Army and Air Force discipline are included.
The term boarding house covers buildings described as such in the Census
returns and any buildings returned as private dwellings but containing five or
more boarders.
At the 1951 Census, private dwellings in which three or more
boarders were enumerated were classified as boarding houses.
The term inhabited denotes actual residence of a person or persons on Census
night.
Uninhabited buildings therefore include not only those which were vacant
on Census night but also those from which the usual occupants were temporarily
absent.
Permanent habitation means normal occupation all the year round.
TYPES OF PRIVATE DWELLINGS
Inhabited private dwellings are classified on the basis of instructions issued
to the Census enumerators and which are summarised as follows:-
(i) A dwelling house means a dwelling of permanent construction which is not
divided into flats and is not a farmhouse.
A prefabricated bungalow provided
by a local authority is included.
(ii) A flat means a completely self-contained dwelling on one or more floors
with a separate entrance from the street or from a common landing or staircase.
It must be possible to move between its rooms internally, without using a
common landing or staircase used by other households.
Also, none of the
household arrangements (see notes on page ix) must be shared with the occupants
of another unit of dwelling accommodation in the building.
Where a building
has been converted for the use of two or more households, the accommodation of
any part is not regarded as a flat unless each part occupied by a householder
has a main door giving access to the whole of the accommodation and unless the
other conditions specified above are satisfied.
(iii) A farmhouse means a dwelling of permanent construction from which the
occupier carries on farming as a main occupation.
In practice, a dwelling
described as a farmhouse on the Census return was classified as such only if
at least one member of the household was engaged in farming.
(iv) Some other type of dwelling means a tent, caravan or other movable dwell-
ing, or such structure as a barn, out-office, hut or shed of wood, corrugated
iron or similar non-traditional material and which, though fixed, is of such a
temporary and insubstantial nature that it cannot be regarded as a permanent
dwelling.
(73488)
vlli


---

## Page 215

TENURE OF PRIVATE DWELLING ACCOMODATION
When completing the Census returns, private householders were required to
indicate, under the following headings, how they occupied their accommodation;
the information is tabulated on the same basis:-
(i) As owner-occupier, including purchase by mortgage.
(ii) By renting from a local or public authority» including the Northern
Ireland Housing Trust, the Sailors' and Soldiers' Land Trust, Housing
Associations and Government Departments.
(iii) By renting from another landlord* and whether rented furnished or
unfurnished.
(iv) In some other way: this category covers accommodation which is neither
owned nor rented by the household, for example, a house or flat occupied free
of rent by virtue of the particular employment of the head or other member of
the household.
In Table 6, a dwelling shared by two or more households is classified accord-
ing to the tenure stated by the main household, the latter being identified on the
basis of a graded list of tenures, owner occupied taking precedence over rented
and rented/unfurnished taking precedence over rented/furnished.
HOUSEHOLDS
A household is a person living alone, or a group of persons living together
and normally partaking of meals together whether in a dwelling or part thereof or
in a hotel, boarding house, hospital, Service establishment or other premises in
which some form of residential accommodation is provided.
A private household is
a household occupying all or part of a house, flat, farmhouse or other type of
dwelling.
A person (or persons) living in the same dwelling as, but not boarding
with, the household is treated as a separate private household; but if the person
usually has at least one meal per day provided by the household he is regarded as
part of the household,
HOUSEHOLD ARRANGEMENTS
The arrangements on which information was collected in respect of each private
household are listed below.
The householder was required to state whether
individual arrangements were used only by the household, shared with another house-
hold or were not available.
(i) Cold water tap refers to a tap within the building, excluding a tap in an
open yard.
(ii) Hot water tap means water piped to a boiler, a tank with immersion heater,
geyser, sink heater, or any form of heating appliance which will allow hot
water to be drawn from a tap within the building*
(iii) Fixed bath means a bath permanently installed with a waste pipe leading
outside the building.
It is immaterial for this purpose whether there is
water piped to it or whether the room where it is installed is used only as
a bathroom.
(iv) Water closet means any water closet, within or attached to the building or
in the yard, emptying into a main sewer, septic tank or cesspool.
It does not
include a chemical closet or earth closet.
(v) Kitchen sink means a sink inside the building, whether in a kitchen or not,
with a drain pipe leading outside the building, and normally used for washing
up.
It need not have water piped into it.
A wash basin used primarily for
personal toilet is excluded.
(vi) Cooking stove or range means any cooking stove, kitchen range or other
fixed grate using gas, electricity or any other fuel, provided there is an
oven.
It does not apply where the only method of cooking is by gas-ring,
hotplate, portable electric or oil oven or by open fire.
(73488)
ix


---

## Page 216

ROOMS
The rooms counted are those normally used by the household for living, eating
or sleeping purposes.
A kitchen if so used is included, but not a scullery which
is used only for washing, cleaning or cooking.
A pantry, cloakroom, landing,
lobby, closet or bathroom, or any warehouse, office, shop or other room used for
non-domestic purposes is also excluded.
In the tables classifying households by rooms occupied, any room shared by two
or more households in the same dwelling is allocated wholly to one (usually the
main) household, ensuring however that the total number of rooms in the dwelling is
not altered.
VALUATION
The figures shown in Table 4 relate to the rating year 1961/62 and have been
supplied by the Commissioner of Valuation.
They are the totals of the valuations
in the various areas before reduction on account of derating, but exclude in
certain cases the valuations of such miscellaneous items as electricity, gas,
trolley bus and water undertakings, telegraphs, telephones and fisheries.
The
total of the excluded valuations is given in a footnote to the table.
Subject to the Local Government (Rating and Finance) Act (Northern Ireland),
1929, as amended by subsequent legislation, agricultural land and buildings are
exempt from all ordinary rates, while industrial and freight-transport heredita-
ments and salmon and eel fisheries are partially derated.
The consequent
deficiency in the yield of rates is made good by way of General Exchequer
contributions.
USUAL ADDRESS
The persons regarded as having their usual addresses in the area of enumeratioi
include: ~
(i) Persons enumerated in their own homes, and those enumerated in and having
a settled residence in private lodgings, boarding houses and hotels.
(ii) Resident domestic staff enumerated at their places of work.
(iii) Persons enumerated in and who were inmates of homes for old people, homes
for terminal care, homes for handicapped persons and homes for persons requir-
ing special care.
(iv) Persons enumerated in chronic sick or psychiatric hospitals and who had
been patients or inmates for six months or more.
(v) Crews enumerated on vessels and usually living aboard ship, except crews
of vessels registered in ports outside the United Kingdom.
(vi) Persons with no fixed addresses and persons for whom usual addresses
were not stated and which could not be assumed from the Census returns.
School children and students living away from home during term time were
required to give their home addresses on the Census return.
Members of
H.M. Forces not enumerated on the special Forces return had to give the
addresses of their married quarters or other home addresses.
In addition to giving their usual addresses on Census day, persons born out-
side the British Isles were also required to state their usual addresses a year
ago, i.e. at 23rd April, 1960.
BIRTHPLACE
A person born in a country which at Census day had ceased to have a separate
existence is regarded as born in the absorbing country, for example, birthplace
stated as Estonia, Latvia or Ukraine is classified as U.S.S.R.
If the birth-
place of an individual was not stated on the Census return, the county or country
of birth was assumed only where this could be done with reasonable certainty from
other entries in the return.
(73488)
x


---

## Page 217

NATIONALITY
A person born outside the British Isles had to state his nationality on the
Census return.
A person born in a British Colony was instructed to enter "British"
on the return,
A citizen of one of the following was instructed to write "British"
or "Commonwealth citizen";- United Kingdom and Colonies, Canada (including
Newfoundland), Australia, New Zealand, The Union of South Africa, India, Pakistan,
Ghana, Ceylon, The Federation of Malaya, The Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland,
The State of Singapore, Nigeria and Cyprus.
RELIGION
In this classification, religious professions which differ only or mainly in
denominational terminology are generally grouped together, and figures for the
group as a whole are shown under the name by which the group is officially or
commonly known.
Thus the heading Presbyterian includes United Presbyterian,
Trinitarian and Church of Scotland.
Church of Ireland is regarded as embracing
both Anglican and Episcopalian except for persons so described who had a usual
address in England; such persons are classified as Church of England.
In keep-
ing with the practice in the 1951 Census Reports, figures for Church of England
and Episcopal Church of Scotland are merged with those for Church of Ireland in
the body of the tables, but their totals are given in a footnote to Table 18.
TERMINAL EDUCATION AGE
At the 1951 Census, information was sought as to whether persons at educa-
tional establishments were attending full-time or part-time.
In 1961, all
persons aged 15 years and over, who at Census day had ceased to receive full-
time education at school, college, university, etc., were required to state the
age at which their full-time education ended.
In this Report, such age is
referred to as the terminal education age.
OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
The economic activity information collected at the Census was in respect of
persons aged 15 years and over, and related to the activity during the week ended
,22nd April, 1961, i.e. the week preceding Census day.
For a person in employment,
particulars were required of his main gainful employment during the week, irres-
pective of whether he was following his usual occupation or of the hours actually
worked; if not in employment or wholly retired at the end of the week, the refer-
ence was to his last full-time employment.
A fourfold classification was adopted in analysing the economic activity
information, namely, by occupation, industry, employment status and economic
position, each being independent of the others and dealing with a different aspect
of a person's employment or former employment.
The results are tabulated on the
basis of the revised occupational and industrial Classifications used for 1961
Census purposes in the rest of the United Kingdom, viz., the Classification of
Occupations, 1960 (H.M.S. 0. Price £1 7s. 6d. net) and the Standard Industrial
Classification, 1958 (H.M.S. 0. Price 2s. 6d. net).
Compared with previous
Classifications, considerable changes have been made in the new Classifications to help
make them conform with the current organisation of industry and commerce and to
bring them more into line with recommended international standard Classifications.
A detailed comparison of the 1961 data with those of earlier Censuses is, therefore,
necessarily limited.
As at the 1951 Census, the sole criterion for determining a person's occupation
is the kind of work he performs.
The nature of the factory, business or service
in which he is employed has no bearing on his occupational classification except to
the extent that it may enable the nature of his duties to be more clearly defined.
Thus every joiner, whether employed in shipbuilding, the building industry or on
routine maintenance work in a textile factory, is classified to the same occupa-
tional group.
The only exception to the rule applies to labourers assigned to
groups 181-188 where the allocation is on a strictly industrial basis.
Similarly,
the industry to which a person is classified is largely independent of his occupation
and is determined by reference to the business or economic activity in, or for the
purposes of, which his occupation is followed, regard being had only to the nature
(73488)
xi


---

## Page 218

of the service or product to which his labour contributes.
For classification
purposes, the industrial unit used is the "establishment", which is normally the
whole of the premises, such as a factory, transport undertaking or shop, at a
particular address.
Brief definitions of certain terms in the Report are given below.
Occupied or economically active population: all persons in employment during
the week preceding Census day, including those temporarily away from work because
of holidays, sickness, strikes, etc.; also included are persons who were out of
employment but expecting to work again and young persons who had left school and
were seeking their first job.
Working population: the occupied or economically active population excluding
persons out of work.
Large establishments: establishments, other than farms, employing 25 or more
persons.
Small establishments: other establishments.
Economically inactive population: all persons aged 15 and over without paid
occupations.
Included are the following specified groups:-
Institution inmates: inmates of institutions who returned a former occupation
and were not stated to be retired, but who were known or assumed to be with-
drawn from employment for a period in excess of six months.
Retired persons: formerly occupied persons who ceased to be employed and did
not expect to work again.
Students: persons above the age of compulsory education at educational
establishments.
Housewives: with no other stated occupation.
SOCIAL CLASS AND SOCIO-ECONOMIC GROUPS
A convenient summary of economic activity information for social and medical
purposes is provided by two further classifications, namely Social Class and Socio-
economic Group.
Social Class - Under this classification, the many occupational unit groups
are arranged into the following five broad categories:-
I. Professional, etc. occupations
II. Intermediate occupations
III. Skilled occupations
IV. Partly skilled occupations
V. Unskilled occupations
To give a somewhat finer classification, social classes II, III and IV are divided
into "manual", "non-manual" and "agricultural" sub-groups.
Within this framework,
social class I is wholly "non-manual", and social class V wholly "manual". Members
of the Armed Forces and persons with inadequately described occupations are
excluded from the classification.
Socio-economic Groups * The 13 socio-economic groups introduced in 1951 are
replaced by 16 rather different groups, the allocation of persons to the groups
being determined by a cross-classification of occupation and employment status.
The socio-economic groups, with brief definitions, are as follows:-
(1) Employers and managers in central and local government, industry,
commerce, etc. - large establishments
(2) Employers and managers in industry, commerce, etc. - small establishments
(3) Professional workers - self employed
Self employed persons engaged in work normally requiring qualifications
of university degree standard.
(73488)
xll


---

## Page 219

(4) Professional workers - employees
Employees engaged in work normally requiring qualifications of
university degree standard.
(5) Intermediate non-manual workers
Employees, not exercising general planning or supervisory powers,
engaged in non-manual occupations ancillary to the professions but not
normally requiring qualifications of university degree standard;
persons engaged in artistic work and not employing others thereat; and
persons engaged in occupations otherwise included in Group (6) who have
an additional and formal supervisory function,
(6)
Junior non-manual workers
Employees, not exercising general planning or supervisory powers,
engaged in clerical, sales and non-manual communications and security
occupations, excluding those who have additional and formal supervisory
functions,
(7)
Personal service workers
Employees engaged in service occupations caring for food,
drink,
clothing and other personal needs.
(8)
Foremen and supervisors - manual
Employees (other than managers) who formally and immediately super-
vise o t h e r s engaged in manual occupations, whether or not themselves
engaged in such occupations.
(9) Skilled manual workers
Employees engaged in manual occupations which require considerable
and specific skills.
(10) Semi-skilled manual workers
Employees engaged in manual occupations which require slight but
specific skills.
(11) Unskilled manual workers
Other employees engaged in manual occupations.
(12) Own account workers (other than professional)
Self employed persons engaged in any trade, personal service or
manual occupation
not normally
requiring
training
of university
degree
standard and having
no employees
other
than family
workers*
(•13) Farmers - employers and managers
Persons who own, rent or manage farms, market gardens or forests,
employing people other than family workers in the work of the enterprise.
(14) Farmers - own account
Persons who own or rent farms, market gardens or forests and having
no employees other than family workers.
(15) Agricultural workers
Employees engaged in tending crops, animals, game or f o r e s t s ,
or
operating a g r i c u l t u r a l or forestry machinery.
(16)
Members of armed forces.
The d e t a i l e d allocations of the occupation/employment s t a t u s groups to
the social c l a s s e s and socio-economic groups are given in the
C l a s s i f i c a t i o n
of Occupations, I960.

## Page 220

(INVENTION AS TO DOTS (.) AND DASHES (-)
In the tables, a dot (.) indicates that figures are not available or that the
column heading is not relevant to the particular line on which the dot is shown;
a dash (-) indicates that the quantity is zero or, in the case of rates, less than
half of the final digit.
COMPLETION AND REVISION OF CENSUS INFORMATION
When collecting
the Census returns,
the enumerators
were required
to examine
them for obvious omissions and errors, asking such questions from the persons
delivering the returns as seemed necessary to complete and correct them.
Further
scrutiny and revision of the returns took place at various stages of the work in
the Census Office.
In some cases, notably where ages had been omitted, enquiries
were instituted; otherwise, information was inserted or amended only if reasonable
assumptions could be made from other particulars on the returns.
Prior to compila-
tion, the data extracted from the returns were edited for impossible and unlikely
combinations, corrections being made by reference back to the returns.
Finally,
the tabulations were checked for compatibility of related data.
(73488)
xiv


---

## Page 221

PART II
STATISTICAL NOTES
The explanatory notes and definitions in Part I apply equally to the notes
which follow and to the tables in Part III.
AREA
The area of the County Borough at Census day was 18,052 acres, including
2,237 acres of tidal and 166 acres of inland water.
Excluding portions covered
by tidal water, the area was 458 acres more than at the 1951 Census.
The
increase occurred in Victoria Ward as the result of reclamation of land from
Belfast Lough.
POPULATION
The total population of the County Borough was 415,856, which is 27,815
persons or 6*3 per cent less than at the 1951 Census.
Table 2 shows the trends since 1821 when the first complete Census of Ireland
was taken.
Between that year and 1851, the population rose steadily from 37,277
to 87,062.
In the next fifty years, a fourfold increase to close on 350,000 took
place, which is attributed to the combined effects of the natural increase in the
population (i.e. excess of births over deaths), borough boundary extensions and a
considerable influx from other parts of Northern Ireland.
From the turn of the
century until 1951 the increase continued but at a declining rate; for the period

    1937-51 it amounted to only 5,585 persons or an average of just under 400 per
annum.

    The reduction of 27,815 during the ten years 1951-61 is the first
population decrease recorded for the County Borough.
The Preliminary Report on the 1961 Census pointed out that the decrease in
population of the County Borough had been more than offset by an increase in the
population of the adjoining administrative areas.
Table 4 of that Report showed
that, in Newtownabbey Urban District and those townlands which wholly or in part
comprised continuous built-up areas extending from the County Borough boundary,
the aggregate increase in population since 1951 had been almost 54,000.
In
other words, despite the decrease within the legal perimeter of Belfast, the
total population of the urbanised area centred on the City increased by over
26,000.
See also the notes on "Belfast and Environs" on page xvii.
Natural Increase: Figures of the natural increase, actual variation and net
outward movement of the population in each intercensal period since 1901 are given
in Table I.
Because of the boundary extension in 1896, figures for the County
Borough as now constituted are not available for periods prior to 1901.

## Page 222

The birth rate in the County Borough for the latest intercensal period was
generally lower than during the war and early post-war years, with the result that
the annual natural increase during the ten years 1951-61 was, on average, below that
for the period 1937-51.
It was also lower than that for other periods since 1901
except 1926-37.
The augmentation of the natural increase by the movement of persons into the
City in the latter half of the nineteenth century was reversed about the end of the
century and, as is seen from Table I, a small net outward movement was recorded for
the period 1901-11.
Each succeeding period shows a movement in the same direction
and mostly of increasing dimensions, the total net outward movement of 64,372
persons in 1951-61 representing the highest annual average for any period covered
by the Table.
Information on the destinations of the persons who moved out is not
available, but many have, no doubt, settled in the new houses in the environs of
the City.

during the period 1951-61.

Decreases occurred in twelve of the fifteen wards and were most pronounced in
those wards which lie in or extend into the central and generally oldest parts of
the City.
St. Anne's ward, in which increases were recorded at every previous

    Census from 1911, showed the largest reduction, viz. 5,090 persons or 16.5 per cent.
The greatest relative decrease was 20.5 per cent in St. George's where the popula-
tion has gradually diminished throughout the past fifty years and is now less than
two-thirds of what it was in 1901.
Other percentage decreases of note were 19.7
in Court, 17.4 in Dock and 15.5 in Smithfield; these three wards .also showed
decreases at the 1937 and 1951 Censuses, and the population of each is considerably
less than at the beginning of the present century.
Of the three wards with a
contrary trend to that of the County Borough as a whole, Falls had an increase of
5.0 per cent which continues the fairly steady growth of population in the ward in
previous intercensal periods.
The other increases recorded were 2.8 per cent in
Woodvale and 1.2 per cent in Shankill.
(73488)
xvi


---

## Page 223

The concentration of population in the County Borough averaged 26.5 persons
per acre compared with 28.9 in 1951.
Ward averages varied from 127.5 in Court
ward to 11.7 in Victoria and were highest in the small wards in and near the City
centre.
Using persons per acre as a rough basis for contrasting densities in
different cities, the figure of 26.5 for Belfast compares with 16.6 for Bristol
and 14.1 for Edinburgh, both of which have total populations not very different
from that of Belfast.
Of the major cities in Great Britain, only Glasgow (27.5)
and Liverpool (26.9) had a higher number of persons per acre than Belfast.
Parliamentary Constituencies: The constituent divisions of the County Borough
for the purpose of elections to the Parliaments of both Northern Ireland and the
United Kingdom are set out in Table 11 together with figures of population, the
number of electors and the ratio of electors per 1,000 population in each
constituency.
For the County Borough as a whole, the numbers of electors with
residence qualifications for Northern Ireland and United Kingdom elections
represented 98.5 per cent and 98. 6 per cent, respectively, of the total population
aged 21 years and over.

## Page 224

While the population within the legal perimeter of the County Borough decreased
by 27,815 or 6.3 per cent since 1951, the total of the County Borough and its
environs increased by 26,014 or 5.2 per cent.
There was, therefore, a net increase
of 53,829 in the population of the environs alone, the bulk of which was accounted
for by more or less equal increases in the figures for Newtownabbey Urban District
and the environs portion of each of Castlereagh and Lisburn Rural Districts.
At
528,700, the population of the urbanised area centred on the City in 1961 was
37 per cent of the Northern Ireland total.
PRIVATE DWELLINGS AND HOUSEHOLDS
Total Dwellings: In 1961 there were 117,502 private dwellings in the County
Borough, of which 114,397 were inhabited and 3,105 were uninhabited.
This
represents an increase of 8,550 dwellings, or 7.8 per cent, on the corresponding

    1951 total of 108,952 (inhabited 107,358; uninhabited 1,594).
The intercensal
variations are shown by wards in Table IV.

In keeping with the general decline in the number of dwellings in areas towards
the City centre - a trend which may be accelerated in future by slum clearance -
decreases were again recorded in the total dwellings figure for each of Court,
St. Anne's, St. George's and Smithfield wards.
The total for Dock, however was
unchanged.
Increases occurred in all other wards, notably Shankill ( + 1,413 dwell-
ings) and Pottinger (+ 1,360).
The increase of 1,511 in the number of uninhabited
dwellings was distributed over all wards in much the same proportions as the total
dwellings.
It should be borne in mind that the total of 3,105 dwellings returned
as uninhabited includes an unspecified number of homes from which the usual
occupants were only temporarily away on Census night.
According to statistics furnished by the Ministry of Health and Local
Government, approximately 9,750 new units of dwelling accommodation were provided
in the County Borough during the intercensal period.
When this figure is compared
with the Census increase of 8,500 in the total inhabited and uninhabited dwellings,
it follows that some 1,200 dwellings recorded in 1951 either had ceased to exist by
1961 or were no longer used for private dwelling purposes.
Inhabited Dwellings: Table 6 shows, in respect of the County Borough and each
ward, the numbers of inhabited private dwellings, rooms and population therein.

During the period 1937-51 the numbers of inhabited dwellings and persons there-
in increased by 6,850 and 9,443, respectively, whereas in the following ten years a
dwellings increase of 7,039 was accompanied by a decrease of 25,755 in the private
household population.
Consequently, there was a considerably greater decline in the
average numbers of persons per dwelling and per room during the last intercensal
period than in the previous period.
At 3.52 persons per dwelling and 0.76 persons
per room, the 1961 average densities were about 25 per cent lower than the correspon-
ding averages in 1926.
Shared dwellings, i,e, those containing more than one house-
hold, also decreased significantly, representing only 0.9 per cent of the total
inhabited dwellings compared with 3.4 per cent in 1951 and 7.6 per cent in 1926,
Of

    the increase of 7,039 in the number of inhabited dwellings, 4,074 or 57.9 per cent
were in the 1-3 roomed group.
Table YI shows for each ward the percentages of inhabited dwellings by room
categories, together with average population densities per dwelling and per room.

Falls ward, in which 76.1 per cent of the inhabited dwellings had less than
five rooms, had the highest average number of persons per dwelling (4.57) and the
second highest average number of persons per room (l.ll).
Smithfield ward, with
(73488)
Xix


---

## Page 226

85.0 per cent of its dwellings in the 1-4 roomed category, had the second highest
number of persons per dwelling (4.El) and the highest number of persons per room
(1.16).
On the other hand, Ormeau ward, in which 59.3 per cent of the dwellings
contained five or more rooms, had the lowest average of 3.17 persons per dwelling
and also a relatively low average of 0.66 persons per room.
The corresponding
averages for Windsor ward, where 66.1 per cent of the dwellings had five or more
rooms, were 3.32 persons per dwelling and 0.60 persons per room, the latter being
the lowest for any ward.
Compared with an average of 4.62 rooms per dwelling for the County Borough
as a whole, Court, St. George's and Smithfield wards had averages of less than
four, while Clifton, Cromac and Windsor had averages of more than five rooms per
dwelling.
Type and Tenure of Inhabited Dwellings: Of the 114,376 inhabited permanent
dwellings, 111,584 or 97.6 per cent were classified as dwelling houses.
Of the

    latter, 37,134 or one-third were owner occupied; 11,474 or 10.3 per cent were
rented from local or public authorities, while 60,680 or 54.4 per cent were
rented unfurnished from other landlords.
Those rented furnished from other land-
lords numbered 1,434, i.e. only 1.3 per cent of the total dwelling houses.
At 2,774, the number of dwellings classed as flats represented 2.4 per cent
of the total dwellings.
Approximately half of the flats were rented from local
or public authorities, and three-quarters,of the remainder were rented from
other landlords in the broad proportions of one-third furnished and two-thirds
unfurnished.
Eighteen dwellings were returned as farmhouses, four being in
Shankill ward and 10 in Woodvale ward.
Table VII shows by wards the percentages of inhabited dwelling houses and
flats in each main tenure category.

Windsor, Cromac, Ormeau, Pottinger and Clifton wards, in that order, had the
highest proportions of owner-occupied dwelling houses, ranging from 46.2 per cent
in Windsor to 40.4 per cent in Clifton; the lowest percentages were in Smithfield

    (11.1), Court (13.4), Dock (14.3), Falls (15.0) and St. Georges (16. l).
Wards
with the highest percentages rented from local or public authorities were Falls

    (20.3), Woodvale (17.6) and Duncairn (16.8); in Court and Dock wards, the propor-
tions were less than 2 per cerit.
Dwelling houses rented from other landlords were
proportionately highest in Court, Dock, St. George's and Snithfield wards where
more than three out of every four houses were tenanted in that way.
(73488)
xx


---

## Page 227

While the relative distribution of flats by wards varied considerably, the
numbers involved in some cases are hardly significant enough to justify detailed
comparisons.
Private Households: Table 7 details the numbers of private households in the
County Borough and each ward by household sizes, rooms occupied and densities of
room occupation.

    There were 116,151 private households in 1961 or 3,561 more
than at the previous Census.
This represents an increase of 3.16 per cent, which
may be contrasted with a 6.56 per cent increase in the number of inhabited private
dwellings, a 5.84 per cent increase in the number of rooms occupied and a decrease
of 6.01 per cent in the private household population.
The previously mentioned
decreases - of between 11 and 12 per cent - in the average numbers of persons per
dwelling and per room were accompanied by a decline from 3.80 to 3.47, or almost
nine per cent, in the average number of persons per household.
In 1937 and 1926,
the average sizes of households were 4.02 and 4.45 persons respectively.
Most of the increase in the total households occurred in the one-person and
two-persons groups; households in the former increased from 9,530 to 14,395, viz.,

    by 51.0 per cent, and those in the two-persons group from 22,725 to 28,547, viz.,
by 25.6 per cent.
Expressed as percentages of the total households, the largest

    groups were: two-persons 24.6; three-persons 21.1; four-persons 17.6; one-
person 12.4 and five-persons 11.0.
In 1951, the three-persons household
(21.3 per cent) was the largest group, followed by two-persons (20.2), four-
persons (19.6), five-persons (13.2) and one-person (8.5).
The 1961 percentage distribution of private households by sizes is shown for
each ward in Table VIII below.

Windsor ward, in which 45.4 per cent of the households consisted of only one
or two persons, had the lowest average of 3.14 persons per household.
Other wards
with averages well below that for the County Borough as a whole were Ormeau (3.15),
St. George's (3.20) and Cromac (3.22).
The highest averages were 4.53 persons in
Falls and 4.14 in Smithfield wards.
In these wards the numbers of households con-
sisting of six or more persons represented 32.2 per cent of the total households in
Falls and 27.6 per cent in Smithfield - percentages which were more than twice the
corresponding percentage for the County Borough.
All wards showed lower average
sizes of households than in 1951.
Rooms and Density of Occupation: In 1961, 2.8 per cent of all private house-
holds in the County Borough occupied one or two rooms, which is less than half of
the corresponding percentage in 1951.
Households occupying three rooms represented

    13.7 per cent compared with 11.7 per cent in 1951.
Those occupying four rooms
(73488)
xxl


---

## Page 228

still formed the largest group, although the percentage dropped from 43.1 to 39.9
during the intercensal period.
Households occupying five or more rooms increased
from 39.0 per cent to 43.6 per cent.
At the 1951 Census, households of three
persons occupying four rooms were in the majority followed by households of four
persons occupying four rooms.
By 1961, households of two persons occupying four
rooms had become the most frequent, with the three-persons households occupying
four rooms in second place.
Table IX shows for each ward the 1961 relative distribution of households by
rooms occupied, together with the percentages of households and population living
at a density of over two persons per room.
(Such a density has been chosen to
provide a convenient comparison with information published at previous Censuses and
not as a measure or definition of overcrowding.
Data for a limited sub-division
of a density of two or less persons per room are given in Table 7; figures for
almost any required density can be arrived at from the details in the Table).
As
households not sharing accommodation with other households accounted for 97.6 per
cent of the total, the distribution of households by rooms occupied was generally
similar to that of private dwellings by rooms therein.
Dock and Smithfield wards

    with percentages of 11.7 and 10.9, respectively, returned the highest proportions
of households living in 1-2 roomed accommodation.
The proportions of households
occupying less than four rooms were highest in Court ward (54.5per cent),
St. George's (52.9 per cent) and Smithfield (52.9 per cent).
Households occupy-
ing more than five rooms were relatively greatest in Clifton (32.1 per cent),
Cromac (34.2 per cent) and Windsor (35.4 per cent).

Households in the County Borough accommodated at a density of over two persons
per room numbered 3,192 or 2.7 per cent of the total households, compared with
4.6 per cent in 1951 and 8.7 per cent in 1926.
The corresponding 1961 proportions
for the wards ranged from 13.1 per cent in Smithfield (which, with Dock ward, had
the highest ratio of 1-2 roomed dwellings) and 9.7 per cent in Falls to 0.8 per cent
in Ormeau and only 0.5 per cent in Windsor (which had the highest ratio of dwell-
ings with over five rooms).
Households at a density of li>-2 persons inclusive per room accounted for 8.5
per cent of the total households; those at one and over but under l|- persons per
room represented 23.9 per cent.
Households returning an average density of less
than one person per room formed 64.9 per cent of the total, as against 55.4 per cent
in 1951.
In the wards, the percentage of households at under one person per room
was lowest in Smithfield (39.7) and highest in Windsor (77.8).
(73488)
xxli


---

## Page 229

The number of persons residing at over two per room constituted 6.8 per cent
of the total private household population, compared with 9.2 per cent in 1951 and
14; 4 per cent in 1926.
A decrease was recorded in the percentage for each ward
except Falls where the proportion at over two persons per room increased slightly
during the last intercensal period from 19.8 per cent to 20.1 per cent which was
the second highest for any ward.
As at previous Northern Ireland Censuses, the
highest ratio was in Smithfield ward where one-quarter of the 1961 population
lived at a density of over two persons per room.
Persons living at densities of l-Jr-2 persons and one and over but under 1-^-
persons per room represented 15.5 per cent and 30.9 per cent, respectively, of the
total private household population.
In the under one person per room category,
the percentage for the County Borough was 46.8; the corresponding percentages for

    the wards ranged from 19.5 in Smithfield and 20.5 in Falls to 60.2 in Cromac, 60.5
in Ormeau and 65.4 in Windsor.
The number of households with three or more persons living in a single room
decreased during the last intercensal period from 1,040 to 151, which indicates a
substantial improvement in one field of serious overcrowding which existed in the
City in the early post-war years.
Household Arrangements: Table 8 shows, in respect of the arrangements on
which information was sought in the Census return, the extent to which private
households had access to such arrangements in their dwellings and whether they had
exclusive use of the arrangements or shared them with other households.
The
information is analysed according to the number of households per dwelling and the
tenure and type of accommodation occupied.
In the County Borough as a whole, 58,438 households or 50.3 per cent had sole
use of all six arrangements and a further 573 households or 0.5 per cent shared all
arrangements with other households.
Households without any of the arrangements
numbered 18, of which seven occupied dwelling houses, one occupied a farmhouse and
10 occupied non-permanent or mobile dwellings.
Almost all households had sole or
shared use of one or more of the following:- cold water tap (99.9 per cent);
water closet (99.6 per cent); kitchen sink (99.2 per cent); cooking stove or
range (99.4 per cent).
The arrangements possessed by fewest households were a hot
water tap and a fixed bath; 47,116 households or 40.6 per cent had no hot water
tap and 55,340 or 47.7 per cent lacked a fixed bath.
Table X shows the numbers of
households, expressed as percentages of the total households, which had use (sole
or shared) of the various arrangements according to the tenure and type of accommoda-
tion occupied.
Of the significant categories, households in accommodation provided
by local or public authorities had relatively the greatest availability of arrange-
ments; for example, 86.9 per cent of such households had use of all six arrangements
compared with 75.6 per cent for households in owner-occupied accommodation and an
average of 50.8 per cent for all households.
The proportion of households with all
or any of the arrangements was generally lowest in the case of those in accommodation
rented unfurnished from private landlords.
As regards the availability of arrange-
ments according to the type of accommodation occupied, households in flats fared best
on the whole.

^Households without a cold water tap In the dwelling represented less than 0.05 per cent.
Households in Clifton, Windsor and Duncairn wards had relatively the highest avail-

    ability, the percentages for households having all arrangements being 72.7, 68.7 and
    65.7, respectively, compared with an average of 50.8 per cent for the County Borough.
In the main, households in the four small wards in and near the City centre, viz.
Smithfield, St. George's Dock and Court wards, had the fewest arrangements at their
disposal, the lack of hot water taps and fixed baths, particularly the latter, being
most evident.
Household Socio-economic Groups: Table 9 analyses, by the socio-economic group
of the head of household, the numbers of private households and persons therein
according to the tenure of the accommodation and the number of rooms occupied by the
household.
Except for that relating to rooms, the information is summarised in
Table XII to provide a convenient comparison between the main groups.
A brief
definition of each socio-economic group is given in Part I of the Report.

1, 2 Employers and managers
3, 4 Professlonal workers

Approximately 70 per cent of the heads of households who were employers and
managers (S.E. G5s 1 and 2) and professional workers (S.E.Gfs 3 and 4) owned their
accommodation, compared with 22.1 per cent for personal service workers (S.E.G. 7),

    20.5 per cent for semi-skilled manual workers (S.E.G. 10) and 15.4 per cent for
unskilled manual workers (S.E.G.ll).
(73488)
xxlv


---

## Page 231

Householders in accommodation rented from local or public authorities accounted
for 1.4 per cent of those in the professional workers' group, 4.7 per cent of
employers and managers and 5.4 per cent of own account workers (S.E.G. 12).
The
percentages for the other groups (excluding S.E.G's 13-16 in which the numbers of
householders were not significant) were fairly close to the average of 11.1 for all
households in local or public authority dwellings.
In the category of householders
who rented their accommodation from other landlords, the proportions ranged from
70.5 per cent for unskilled manual workers and some 65 per cent for semi-skilled
manual workers and personal service workers to 23.9 per cent for employers and
managers and 20.7 per cent for professional workers.
Households in accommodation occupied in some other way represented only 1.0 per
cent of the total households.
The proportion for each socio-economic group was
also relatively small except in the professional workers' group where it reached
8.0 per cent; a probable reason for the latter is that the group concerned
includes clergymen, many of whom occupied rent-free accommodation provided by their
church authorities.
Table XII also compares the average sizes of households by socio-economic
groups.
Against an overall average of 3.47 persons, the averages for households
in the unskilled and skilled manual workers' groups were highest at 3.95 and 3.75
persons, respectively; the lowest averages were 3.00 persons for households of
which the heads were personal service workers and 3.16 persons for those in the
intermediate and junior non-manual workers' groups (S.E.G's 5 and 6).
With
regard to households in the residual category of "Indefinite and not applicable",
it is pointed out that such households include those of which the heads had no
occupation other than that of housewife and who were accordingly excluded from the
socio-economic classification; in many cases, the household heads were either
widows or married women whose husbands were away on Census night.
Calculations based on the data in Table 9 enable a general comparison to be
made of the average sizes of households by tenure of the accommodation occupied as
follows:-
Tenure
Average size of household
Owner-occupied

3.36
"
It will be seen that the average household in accommodation rented from local or
public authorities consisted of approximately one more person than the average
number of persons per household in each of the other main tenure categories.
USUAL ADDRESS
Table 12 discloses that 98.4 per cent of the enumerated population had usual
addresses in the County Borough.
Of the remainder, 3.761 persons or 0.9 per cent
of the total enumerated population normally lived elsewhere in Northern Ireland,
and 2,887 or 0.7 per cent were usually resident outside Northern Ireland.
SEX, AGE AND MARITAL CONDITION
The population of the County Borough was distributed by sex, age and marital
condition as shown in Table 13.
Summaries for each ward are given in Table 14.
Table XIII compares the position, on an age group basis, with that at the
1951 Census.

    Sex Distribution: In 1961 there were 1,120- females to every 1,000 males com-
    pared with 1,117 per 1,000 in 1951.
Males exceeded females at all ages up to
15 years but thereafter females predominated, the disparity being most pronounced
at the higher ages.
In the combined groups aged 70 and over, there were approxi-
mately nine women to every five men.
Ages: Despite the substantial overall decrease in population, increases were
recorded in the numbers of males and females in each quinquennial age group from

    55 upwards except for a decrease, possibly still reflecting losses in the 1914*18
war, in males at ages 70-79.
There has, in fact, been an acceleration of the trend
for the proportion of older persons to increase.
Eight persons were returned as

    aged 100 years and over, compared with only one widow aged 100 at the 1951 Census.
The movement of growing families to the new houses in the environs of the City
during the intercensal period was, no doubt, a contributory factor in the reduced

    numbers at ages under 10 and in the group 20-44 years.
The changed pattern of the percentage distribution of the population by four
broad age groups since 1901 is indicated below.

Marital Condition: Since 1951, the number of single persons aged 16 (the
statutory minimum age of marriage) and over decreased by 14,647, of which 6*490
were males and 8,157 were females.
Although the total married persons showed a
(73488)
xxvl


---

## Page 233

    loss of 4,667 (1,704 males and 2,963 females), the numbers of married persons of
each sex increased at most ages up to 24 years and after 45 years.
Married males

    aged 17 to 24 increased by 1,115 and married females aged 16 to 23 increased by 674.
The number of widowers decreased by 1,166 whilst widows increased by 1,118.
Of
divorced persons who had not remarried, males increased from 248 to 268 and females
from 414 to 543.

BIRTHPLACE AND NATIONALITY
Table 15 shows the population of the County Borough by sex and place of birth.
Of the total with stated place of birth, 76*5 per cent were born in the County
Borough, 8.9 per cent in the adjacent counties of Antrim and Down and a further
5.8 per cent in the rest of Northern Ireland.
The percentages for persons born in
the Irish Republic and Great Britain (including the Isle of Man and Channel Islands)
were 3.2 and 4.6, respectively.
Only 1.0 per cent were returned as born outside
the British Isles.
Changes in the general pattern at each Census since 1861 are
outlined in Table XIV.

In general, the number of persons born outside Northern Ireland has not had an
appreciable effect on the religious distribution of the total population of the
County Borough.
Table 17 analyses the number of persons born outside the British Isles by sex
and usual address on 23rd April, 1960, distinguishing between British or
Commonwealth citizens and persons of alien nationality.
Of the 4,191 persons with
birthplaces outside the British Isles, 76.1 per cent were returned as British or
Commonwealth citizens, 22.3 per cent were aliens and the remaining 1. 6 per cent
omitted to answer the nationality question.
The British or Commonwealth citizens
were fairly evenly divided as between the sexes, whereas there were slightly more
than three times as many males as females among foreign nationals.
RELIGION
Table 18 shows the population by sex for each ward under four main religious
headings, viz., Roman Catholic, Presbyterian, Church of Ireland and Methodist, and
a fifth group embracing all other and not stated denominations.
Totals for each
denomination in the last-named category with 10 or more adherents are given in a
footnote to the Table.
Changes in the position in the County Borough as a whole
since 1861 are set out in Table XVI below.

    *Includlng Church of England and Episcopal Church of Scotland numbering 3,458 and 20, respectively, In 1961.
In relation to the total population, Roman Catholics accounted for 27.5 per
cent which is the highest ratio at any Census since 1881.
While Presbyterians
still formed the largest single denomination, their proportion fell from
(73488)
xxvin*


---

## Page 235

    30.4 per cent in 1951 to 28.9 in 1961 in continuance of the generally downward trend
revealed at previous Censuses.
The percentage for Church of Ireland was 27.0 com-

    pared with 29.7 and 32.0 in 1951 and 1937 respectively.
At 7.9, the percentage for
Methodists was a slight increase on the 1951 ratio; and the proportion for the other
and not stated denominations rose from 6,2 to 8.7 per cent.
However, comparison
with earlier years is vitiated, to some extent, by the increase from 2,457 to 12,177
in the number of persons who did not state a religious profession - an increase which
probably reflects a much greater awareness in 1961 that it was not obligatory to
answer the Census question on religion.
Table 19 shows the numbers of males and females in each of the main denomina-
tions by individual years of age to 21 and by quinquennial groups from 20 years
onwards.
Between 1951 and 1961, the number of persons returned as Roman Catholics
under 10 years of age increased by 4.1 per cent, while that for all others in the
same age group decreased by 18.2 per cent.
From 10 to 21 years, an increase o f
4.1 per cent was also recorded for Roman Catholics compared with a decrease of 2.4
per cent for all others.
At ages over 21, decreases of 4.4 and 7.3 per cent
occurred in the numbers of Roman Catholics and all others, respectively.
It is not
known how much of this changing pattern of the distribution of population by
religious groups is due to the movement of families to areas outside the
County Borough.
The percentages of persons by religions in certain age groups to the total popu-
lation in those groups, with comparable figures for 1951, are given in Table XVII.

While the number of Roman Catholics at all ages averaged 27.5 per cent o f the
total population, the proportions in the age groups under 25 were substantially
higher;
those in the groups from 25 years upwards were below the average.
The
position was reversed in each of the other main religious categories.
The Roman
Catholic population not only remains the most youthful but tends to have an increas-
ing proportion of young persons in relation to the other denominations.
TERMINAL EDUCATION AGE
Table 20 shows the distribution of the population aged 15 years and over by
terminal education age.
Figures are given by sexes and by individual years of age
from 15 to 24 and age groups thereafter.
As an indication of the relationship
between education and occupation, the information is also analysed by Occupation
Orders in Table 21.
The figures in the "Not applicable" column of Table 20 relate
mainly to students (5,405 males and 4,288 females);
the balance (52 males and 58
females) represents persons who, through infirmity or for other reasons, never had
any full-time education.
(73488)
xx lx

the sexes.

In comparing the figures for individual age groups, regard should be had to
changes which have taken place in the minimum school leaving age.
Although many
children previously remained at school until about 14 years of age, attendance to
that age became compulsory in the County Borough in 1924.
This accounts in some
measure for the relatively substantial decrease in the numbers of persons who left

    school under 14 years of age in the Census age group 45-54 compared with the older
age groups.
Similarly, the raising of the school leaving age to 15 years in 1957
affected persons in the age group 15 to 19, thus tending to vitiate comparison
between that group and the remaining groups.
It should also be noted that the

    figures in the age groups 15-19 and 20-24 cover only persons whose full-time educa-
tion had ceased and thus exclude considerable numbers still in full-time attendance
at educational establishments; 21.8 per cent of the boys and 18.9 per cent of the
girls aged 15-19 were in this excluded category, the corresponding percentages for
the group 20-24 years being 8.8 and 4.6 respectively.
(73488)
XXX

In the case of males, 89.1 per cent of labourers not elsewhere classified
(Order XVIII) had left school before 15 years of age.
Other high corresponding
proportions were painters and decorators (XVI) 84.6 per cent; construction workers
(XV) 84.3 per cent; and engineering and allied trades workers not elsewhere classi-
fied (VII) 80.1 per cent.
Among the largely non-manual workers, 45.2 per cent of
sales workers (XXII), 59.9 per cent of clerical workers (XXI), 72.0 per cent of
administrators and managers (XXIV) and 84.5 per cent of professional, technical
workers, artists (XXV) continued their full-time education until 15 years of age or
later.
Of those in Orders XXIV and XXV, 10.8 per cent and 43.2 per cent,
respectively, had terminal education ages of 20 and over.
As regards females, 89.5 per cent of textile workers (Order X) and 81.6 per
cent of service, sport and recreation workers (XXIII) had terminal education ages
under 15 years.
Three out of every four clothing workers (XI), food, drink and
tobacco workers (XII) and warehousewomen, storekeepers, packers, bottlers (XX) had
likewise left school before reaching 15.
On the other hand, 77.6 per cent of
clerical workers (XXI) and 90.2 per cent of professional, technical workers,
artists (XXV) remained full-time at educational establishments until at least
15 years.
(73488)
xxxi


---

## Page 238

OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
In comparing the economic activity information in this Report with that from
earlier Censuses, it should be borne in mind that the 1961 information was
collected in respect of persons aged 15 years and over, i.e. those who had reached
the minimum school leaving age, whereas the corresponding limits adopted at the 1951

    and 1926 Censuses were 14 and 12 years, respectively.
Questions on occupations and
industries were not asked at the 1937 Census.
Occupations: Table 22 gives figures of males and females aged 15 years and
over, distinguishing between the occupied or economically active population (includ-
ing persons temporarily out of work) and the economically inactive.
The occupied
population figures are analysed.by the full list of occupation Orders and groups
and by industrial status.
In Table 23, the occupied population is further analysed
by sex, marital condition and age.

    Of the 307,401 persons aged 15 years and over in the County Borough, 192,458 or
62.6 per cent were occupied, the percentage for males being 87.0 and for females
42.0.
These percentages were slightly lower than the corresponding 1951 ratios
based on the population aged 14 years and over.

Table XX compares the intercensal changes by broad age groups.
With the grow-
ing emphasis on higher education,
the proportions
occupied in the age groups 25-19
and 20-24 years,
notably
the former,
showed substantial
decreases.
The
largest
relative increase was from 30.1 per cent to 37.3 per cent in the case of females

    aged 45-64, which reflects a greater number of married women at work in 1961 than in
1951.

    At ages 65 and over, the percentage of males occupied fell from 35.0 to 29.3,
and that for females from 6,8 to 6.2.
It may be noted that the 1961 percentages for
occupied males and females aged 65 years and over were both less than half of the
corresponding ratios at the 1926 Census.
In relation to the total persons (including children under the minimum school
leaving age), the occupied population represented 46.3 per cent compared with 47.8
per cent in 1951.
The proportion for males decreased from 30.7 to 29.5 per cent
and for females from 17.1 to 16.8 per cent.
Anong occupied males, 33.0 per cent were single, 64.4 per cent were married and

    2.6 per cent were widowed and divorced, compared with 37.1, 59.8 and 3.1 per cent,
respectively, in 1951.
The percentages for females were:- single 60.4 (1951:-

    69.4), married 32.7 (25.2) and widowed and divorced 6.9 (5.4).
Some 62 per cent of
the total occupied married women were employed as textile workers (Order X), cloth-
ing workers (Order XI) and service, sport and recreation workers (Order XXIII).
In
these Orders the proportions of married women to total females were:- textile
workers 48.1 per cent, clothing workers 31.2 per cent and service, sport and
recreation workers 47.4 per cent.
The following statement sets out the percentage distribution of the occupied
population by industrial status.

More than half of the occupied males were confined to four Orders, viz., VII
(Engineering and allied trades workers n. e.c.) 18.2 per cent, XVTII (Labourers
n.e.c. ) 14.7 per cent, XIX (Transport and communications workers) 10.4 per cent and
XXII (Sales workers) 8.7 per cent.
In the case of occupied females, more than
three-quarters were classified under only five Orders:- XXIII (Service, sport and
recreation workers) 19.4 per cent, XXI (Clerical workers) 19.3 per cent, XI
(Clothing workers) 16.3 per cent, X (Textile workers) 12.5 per cent and XXII (Sales
workers) 11. 0 per cent.
Social Classes and Socio-economic Groups: Table 24 analyses the population
aged 15 years and over by social class, socio-economic group and age group.
The
social class analysis applies only to occupied and retired persons excluding
members of the armed forces and persons with inadequately described occupations.
In the socio-economic group section of the Table, the heading "Indefinite" relates
(73488)
xxxill


---

## Page 240

to persons with inadequately described occupations.
The "Not applicable" heading
covers students and persons economically inactive other than institution inmates
and retired persons, those in the two last named categories being classified as
appropriate to the socio-economic groups.
The percentage distribution of the persons allocated to the social classes
was as shown below.
I. Professional, etc. occupations
II. Intermediate occupations
III. Skilled occupations
IV. Partly skilled occupations
V. Unskilled occupations
Within the framework of the classification, 71.1 per cent of the males were
manual workers, 28.5 per cent were non-manual and 0.4 per cent were agricultural
workers.

    The percentages for females were:- manual 52.4, non-manual 47.5 and
agricultural 0.1.

Note:- Figures are given only for Orders showing proportions of 5 or more per 1,000 working males or
females.
Orders III to XVI inclusive form the broad group of manufacturing industries.
They accounted for 43.4 per cent of the total working population, the proportions
for males and females being 42.2 and 45.5 per cent respectively of the number of
working persons of each sex.
Order XX, Distributive trades, in which males out-
numbered females by approximately three to two, was the largest individual Order,
accounting for 15.5 per cent of the total working persons.
It was followed by
Order X, Textiles, in which there were over five females to every two males and
which made up 10.1 per cent of the working population.
(73488)

## Page 310

## Page 311

INTRODUCTION
In accordance with the Census Act (Northern Ireland), 1960, a Census of the
population was taken on the 23rd April, 1961, which was also the date of Censuses
in the other parts of the United Kingdom.
It was the fourth since the establish-
ment of the Government of Northern Ireland, previous Censuses having been taken in

    1926, 1957 and 1951.
The 1961 Census was much fuller, as regards the number and scope of the
questions, than any of its predecessors.
Compared with the 1951 enumeration, the
basic questions were much the same but enquiries about nationality, education,
occupation and employment were substantially modified.
New questions asked for
information concerning persons with certain scientific and technological qualifica-
tions, the number of children of women who were or had been married, the type of
accommodation occupied by private households, whether such accommodation was owned
or rented and the extent to which piped water, cooking and toilet facilities were
available.
In November, 1961, a Preliminary Report was published giving population
figures for each county, county borough and administrative area, together with
information on the ages and religious denominations of the people by counties and
county boroughs, and the numbers of private dwellings and households in each
administrative area.
A new Topographical Index, replacing the edition issued in
the series of Reports on the 1926 Census, was published in January, 1963.
This Report is the fourth of seven county volumes to be published on the
1961 Census.
The statistics therein supersede those in the Preliminary Report
and may be accepted as final.
It is generally consistent in design ana scope
with the Reports already published for Belfast County Borough, County Antrim
and County Armagh and the remaining county volumes will be in similar form.
Although details of population and habitable buildings in respect of townlands
in rural districts are not given in the county volumes, the figures, for which
a charge may be made depending on the extent of the infoimation required, are
available on application to the General Register Office.
A General Report will follow the county volumes.
It will summarize for the
whole country the information in the county volumes and, in some respects, deal
with it in greater detail.
The General Report will also contain certain additional
information, including that on persons with scientific and technological qualifica-
tions, which has not been analysed on a county basis.
There will be a separate
Report on the Fertility of Marriage.
General Register Office,
Fermanagh House,
Ormeau Avenue,
Belfast, 2.
December,
1963,
