## Page 315

PART I
EXPLANATORY NOTES AND DEFINITIONS OF
TERMS USED IN THE REPORT
AREA
The acreages given are as measured by the Ordnance Survey and relate to the
County and its administrative and other areas as constituted at the date of the
1961 Census.
Foreshore is excluded.
Acreages of areas covered by the larger
rivers, lakes and tideways are also excluded from the body of Tables 4 and 5, but
the total is given in a footnote thereto.
POPULATION
The 1961 Census population comprises all living persons, irrespective of their
usual address or nationality, who spent Census night in the area of enumeration.
Members of the Armed Forces (including those of Commonwealth and foreign countries
except personnel aboard foreign naval vessels) are included.
Persons travelling
on Census night were enumerated with the population of the district in which they
arrived the next day unless they had already been enumerated elsewhere.
Persons
on vessels at moorings or anchorage in Northern Ireland waters or aboard ships on
coastwise voyage to Northern Ireland ports were coiinted as part of the population
of the district containing the mooring, anchorage or arrival port.
Population figures for the 1951 Census are adjusted to relate to areas as con-
stituted at the date of the 1961 Census.
Figures for earlier Censuses relate,
generally, to areas as constituted at the respective Census dates.
AEMINISTOATIVE DIVISIONS
The County is divided into nineteen administrative areas - two municipal
boroughs, nine urban districts and eight rural districts.
The municipal boroughs
and four of the urban districts are divided into wards which form the units for
local council elections; in each of the remaining five urban districts, the entire
district is the unit for municipal elections.
In rural districts, the units are
the district electoral divisions of which there are 122.
The County is also
divided into twenty county electoral divisions which are the units for county
council election purposes.
There are eight parliamentary constituencies for elections to the Northern
Ireland Parliament and two constituencies for elections to the United Kingdom
Parliament.
The following changes, affecting Census statistics, took place in the name,
constitution or boundaries of administrative areas during the intercensal period:-
(1) The name of Nevrtownards Rural District was changed to North Down Rural
District (1st August, 1954).
(2) A portion of North Down Rural District was transferred to Bangor
Municipal Borough (1st April, 1956).
Since the Census was taken, the name of Do^vnpatrick Rural District was changed to
East Down Rural District (1st January, 1962); the latter name is used in this
Report.
BUILDINGS FOR HABITATION
For eni;mieration record purposes, a building was broadly defined as a structure
comprising one or more rooms or other spaces enclosed within external or party walls.
A structure detached from but subsidiary to a main building was regarded as part of
the main building unless it contained residential accommodation normally occupied by
(86963A)
vll


---

## Page 316

a separate household.
Non-pei*manent structures such as huts, shacks and converted
railway carriages and mobile structures such as tents and caravans were treated as
buildings only if they were occupied on Census night or were some person*s usual
residence.
Unoccupied structures of wood, asbestos, corrugated iron, etc., used as
casual residences, e.g, at week-ends or holiday times, were regarded as buildings
if they appeared to be of sound construction and had amenities such as piped water,
sewerage facilities and electric lighting.
Dilapidated structures which were unoccupied and unlikely to be used again were
ignored.
Buildings in course of erection were listed only if some part thereof was
in use as residential accommodation.
Buildin<g:s for habitation comprise all buildings used wholly or partly as
residential accommodation.
They are divided into two categories:-
(i) Private dwellings consisting of structurally separate units of living
accommodation occupied or normally occupied by private households.
They
include houses, flats, non-permanent and mobile structures, and accommodation
in buildings which are not wholly residential.
Married quarters in Service
establishments are also included.
A building is regarded as not wholly residential if part of it is used
exclusively or primarily for pxirposes other than living accommodation, for
example, a factory or block of offices containing a director's or caretaker's
flat, or a building comprising a shop and dwelling.
Buildings such as a
doctor's or dentist's house with a room or annexe used as a sxxrgery and a
house containing a clergyman's or author's study are, however, treated as
wholly residential.
(ii) Other buildings such as hotels, hostels, boarding houses, hospitals,
vjelfare centres, religious community premises, boarding schools and other
premises which exist for a specific functional purpose in addition to pro-
viding residential accommodation.
Civilian shipping and establishments under
Naval, Army and Air Force discipline are included.
The term boarding house covers buildings described as such in the Census
returns and any buildings returned as private dwellings but containing five or
more boarders.
At the 1951 Census, private dwellings in which three or more
boarders were enumerated were classified as boarding houses.
The term inhabited denotes actual residence of a person or persons on Census
night.
Uninhabited buildings therefore include not only those which were vacant
on Census night but also those from which the usual occupants were temporarily
absent.
Permanent habitation means normal occupation all the year round.
TYPES OF PRIVATE DWELLINGS
Inhabited private dwellings are classified on the basis of instructions issued
to the Census enumerators and which are summarised as follows:-
(i) A dwellinis: house means a dwelling of permanent construction which is not
divided into flats and is not a farmhouse.
A prefabricated bungalow provided
by a local authority is included.
(ii) A flat means a completely self-contained dwelling on one or more floors
with a separate entrance from the street or from a common landing or staircase.
It must be possible to move between its rooms internally, without using a
common landing or staircase used by other households.
Also, none of the
household arrangements (see notes on page ix) must be shared with the occupants
of another unit of dwelling accommodation in the building.
Where a building
has been converted for the use of two or more households, the accommodation of
any part is not regarded as a flat unless each part occupied by a householder
has a main door giving access to the whole of the accommodation and unless the
other conditions specified above are satisfied.
(iii) A farmhouse means a dwelling of permanent construction from which the
occupier carries on farming as a main occupation.
In practice, a dwelling
described as a farmhouse on the Census return was classified as such only if
at least one member of the household was engaged in farming.
{86963A)
vlli


---

## Page 317

(iv) Some other type of dwelling means a tent, caravan
or other movable dwell-
ing, or such structure as a barn, out-office, hut or shed of wood, corrugated
iron or similar non-traditional material and which, though fixed, is of such a
temporary and insubstantial nature that it cannot be regarded as a permanent
dwelling.
TENURE OF PRIVATE DWELLING
AOCa/mDknm
When completing the Census returns, private householders were required to
indicate, under the following headings, how they occupied their accommodation;
the information is tabulated on the same basis:-
(i) As owner-occupier, including purchase by mortgage.
(ii) By renting from a local or public authority, including the Northern
Ireland Housing Trust, the Sailors* and Soldiers' Land Trust, Housing
Associations and Gk)vernment Departments.
(iii) By rentints; from another landlord, and whether rented furnished or
unfurnished.
(iv) In some other way: , this category covers accommodation which is neither
owned nor rented by the household, for example, a house or flat occupied free
of rent by virtue of the particular employment of the head or other member of
the household.
In Table 6, a dwelling shared by tv^o or more households is classified accord-
ing to the tenure stated by the main household, the latter being identified on the
basis of a graded list of tenures, owner occupied taking precedence over rented
and rented/unfurnished taking precedence over rented/furnished.
HOUSEHOLDS
A household is a person living alone, or a group of persons living together
and normally partaking of meals
together
whether in a dwelling or part thereof or
in a hotel, boarding house, hospital, Service establishment or other premises in
which some form of residential accommodation is provided.
A private household is
a household occupying all or part of a dwelling house, flat, farmhouse or other type
of dwelling.
A person (or persons) living in the same dwelling as, but not boarding
with, the household is treated as a separate private household; but if the person
usually has at least one meal per day provided by the household he is regarded as
part of the household.
HOUaEHDLD ARRANGEMENTS
The arrangements on which information vjas collected in respect of each private
household are listed below.
The householder was required to state whether
individual arrangements were used only by the household, shared with another house-
hold or were not available.
(i) Cold water tap refers to a tap within the building, excluding a tap in an
open yard.
(ii) Hot water tap means water piped to a boiler, a tank with immersion heater,
geyser, sink heater, or any form of heating appliance which will allow hot
water to be drawn from a tap within the building.
(iii) Fixed bath means a bath permanently installed with a waste pipe leading
outside the building.
It is immaterial for this purpose whether there is
water piped to it or whether the room where it is installed is used only as
a bathroom.
(iv) Water closet means any water closet, within or attached to the building or
in the yard, emptying into a main sewer, septic tank or cesspool.
It does not
include a chemical closet or earth closet.
(86963A)
Ix


---

## Page 318

(v) Kitchen sink means a sink inside the building, whether in a kitchen or not,
with a drain pipe leading outside the building, and normally used for washing
up.
It need not haye water piped into it.
A v^ash basin used primarily for
personal toilet is excluded.
(vi) Cooking: stove or rauisie means any cooking stove, kitchen range or other
fixed grate using gas, electricity or any other fuel, provided there is an
oven*
It does not apply where the only method of cooking is by gas-ring,
hotplate, portable electric or oil oven or by open fire,
ROOMS
The rooms counted are those normally used by the household for living, eating
or sleeping purposes.
A kitchen if so used is included, but not a scullery which
is used only for washing, cleaning or cooking.
A pantry, cloakroom, landing,
lobby, closet or bathroom, or any warehouse, office, shop or other room used for
non-domestic purposes is also excluded.
In the tables classifying households by rooms occupied, any room shared by two
or more households in the same dwelling is allocated wholly to one (usually the
main) household, ensuring however that the total number of rooms in the dwelling is
not altered,
VALUATION
The figures shown in Tables 4 and 5 relate to the rating year 1961/62 and have
been supplied by the Commissioner of Valuation.
They are the totals of the valuations
in the various areas before reduction on account of derating, but exclude in certain
cases the valuations of such miscellaneous items as electricity, gas, trolley bus and
water undertakings, telegraphs, telephones and fisheries.
The total of the excluded
valuations is given in a footnote to the tables.
Subject to the Local Government (Rating and Finance) Act (Northern Ireland),
1929, as amended by subsequent legislation, agricultural land and buildings are
exempt from all ordinary rates, while industrial and freight-transport hereditaments
and salmon and eel fisheries are partially derated.
The consequent deficiency in
the yield of rates is made good by way of General Exchequer contributions.
USUAL ADDRESS
The persons regarded as having their usual addresses in the area of enumeration
include:-
(i) Persons enumerated in their own homes, and those enumeratea in and having
a settled residence in private lodgings, boarding houses and hotels.
(ii) Resident domestic staff enumerated at their places of work.
(iii) Persons enumerated in and who were inmates of homes for old people, homes
for terminal care, homes for handicapped persons and homes for persons requir-
ing special care.
(iv) Persons enumerated in chronic sick or psychiatric hospitals and who had
been patients or inmates for six months or more.
(v) Crews enumerated on vessels and usually living aboard ship, except crews
of vessels registered in ports outside the United Kingdom.
(vi) Persons with no fixed addresses and persons for whom usual addresses
were not stated and which could not be assiomed from the Census returns.
School children and students living away from home during term time were
required to give their home addresses on the Census return.
Members of
H.M. Forces not enumerated on the special Forces return had to give the addresses
of their married quarters or other home addresses.
In addition to giving their usual addresses on Census day, persons born out-
side the British Isles virere also required to state their usual addresses a year
ago, i.e. at 23rd April, 1960.
{86963A)
X


---

## Page 319

BIKfflPLACE
A person born in a country which at Census day had ceased to have a separate
existence is regarded as born in the absorbing country, for example, birthplace
stated as Estonia, Latvia or Ukraine is classified as U. S. S.R.
If the birth-
place of an individual was not stated on the Census return, the county or country
of birth was assumed only where this could be done with reasonable certainty from
other entries in the return.
NATKBALIIY
A person born outside the British Isles had to state his nationality on the
Census return.
A person born in a British Colony was instructed to enter "British"
on the return.
A citizen of one of the following was instructed to write "British"
or "Commonwealth citizen";- United Kingdom and Colonies, Canada (including
Newfoundland), Australia, New Zealand, The Union of South Africa, India, Pakistan,
Ghana, Ceylon, The Federation of Malaya, The Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland,
The State of Singapore, Nigeria and Cyprus,.
RELIGION
In this classification, religious professions which differ only or mainly in
denominational terminology are generally grouped together, and figures for the
group as a whole are shown under the name by which the group is officially or
commonly known.
Thus the heading Presbyterian includes United Presbyterian,
Trinitarian and Church of Scotland.
Church of Ireland is regarded as embracing
both Anglican and Episcopalian except for persons so described who had a usual
address in England; such persons are classified as Church of England.
In keep-
ing v\fith the practice in the 1951 Census Reports, figures for Church of England
and Episcopal Church of Scotland are merged with those for Church of Ireland in
the body of the tables, but their totals are given in a footnote to Table 18.
TEBMINAL EDUCATION AGE
At the 1951 Census, information was sought as to whether persons at educa-
tional establishments were attending full-time or part-time.
In 1961, all
persons aged 15 years and over, who at Census day had ceased to receive full-
time education at school, college, university, etc., were required to state the
age at which their ^ull-time education ended.
In this Report, such age is
referred to as the terminal education a^e.
OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
The economic activity information collected at the Census was in respect of
persons aged 15 years and over, and related to the activity during the week ended
22nd April, 1961, i.e. the week preceding Census day.
For a person in employment,
particulars were required of his main gainful employment during the week, irres-
pective of whether he was following his usual occupation or of the hours actually
worked; if not in employment or wholly retired at the end of the week, the refer-
ence was to his last full-time employment.
A fourfold classification was adopted in analysing the economic activity
information, namely, by occupation, industry, employment status and economic
position, each being independent of the others and dealing with a different aspect
of a person's employment or former employment.
The results are tabulated on the
basis of the revised occupational and industrial Classifications used for 1961
Census purposes in the rest of the United Kingdom, viz., the Classification of
Occupations, 1960 (H.M.S.O. Price £1 7s. 6d. net) and the Standard Industrial
Classification, 1958 (H.M.S.O. Price 2s. 6d. net).
Compared with previous
Classifications, considerable changes have been made in the new Classifications to
help make them conform with the current organisation of industry and commerce and to
bring them more into line with recommended international standard Classifications.
A detailed comparison of the 1961 data with those of earlier Censuses is,
therefore^
necessarily limited.
As at the 1951 Census, the sole criterion for determining a person's occupation
is the kind of work he performs.
The nature of the factory, business or service
in which he is employed has no bearing on his occupational classification except to
{86963A)
xi


---

## Page 320

the extent that it may enable the nature of his duties to be more clearly defined.
Thus every joiner, whether employed in shipbuilding, the building industry or on
routine maintenance work in a textile factory, is classified to the same occupa-
tional group.
The only exception to the rule applies to labourers assigned to
groups 181-188 where the allocation is on a strictly industrial basis.
Similarly,
the industry to which a person is classified is largely independent of his occupation
and is determined by reference to the business or economic activity -in, or for the
piorposes of, which his occupation is followed, regard being had only to the nature
of the service or product to which his labour contributes.
For classification
purposes, the industrial unit used is the "establishment", which is normally the
whole of the premises, such as a factory, transport undertaking or shop, at a
particular address.
Brief definitions of certain terms in the Report are given below.
Occupied or economically active populations all persons in employment during
the week preceding Census day, including those temporarily away from work because
of holidays, sickness, strikes, etc.; also included are persons who were out of
employment but expecting to work again and young persons who had left school and
were seeking their first job.
Working population: the occupied or economically active population excluding
persons out of work.
Large establishments: establishments, other than farms, employing 25 or more
persons.
Small establishments: other establishments.
Economically inactive population: all persons aged 15 and over without paid
occupations.
Included are the following specified groups:-
Institution inmates: inmates of institutions who returned a former occupation
and were not stated to be retired, but who were kno'&m or assumed to be with-
drawn from employmient for a period in excess of six months.
Retired persons: formerly occupied persons who ceased to be employed and did
not expect to work again,.
Students: persons above the age of compulsory education at educational
e st ab1i shment s.
Housewives: with no other stated occupation.
SOCIAL aASS AND SOaO-EOQNOMIC (MXJPS
A convenient summary of economic activity information for social and medical
purposes is proviaed by two further classifications, namely Social Class and Socio-
economic Group.
Social Class - Under this classification, the many occupational unit groups
are arranged into the following five broad categories:-
I. Professional, etc. occupations
II. Intermediate occupations
III. Skilled occupations
IV. Partly skilled occupations
Y. Unskilled occupations
To give a somewhat finer classification, social classes II, III and IV are divided
into "manual", "non-manual" and "agricultural" sub-groups.
Within this framework,
social class I is wholly "non-manual", and social class V wholly "manual".
Members
of the Armed Forces and persons with inadequately described occupations are
excluded from the classification.
(869e3A)
Xll


---

## Page 321

Socio-economic Groups - The 13 socio-economic groups introduced in 1951 are
replaced by 16 rather different groups, the allocation of persons to the groups
being determined by a cross-classification of occupation and employment status.
The socio-economic groups, with brief definitions, are as follows:-
(1) Employers and managers in central and local government, industry,
commerce, etc. - large establishments
(2) Employers and managers in industry, commerce, etc. - small establishments
(3) Professional workers - self employed
Self employed persons engaged in work normally requiring qualifications
of university degree standard,
(4) Professional workers - employees
Employees engaged in work normally requiring qualifications of
university degree standard.
(5) Intermediate non-manual workers
Employees, not exercising general planning or supervisory powers,
engaged in non-manual occupations ancillary to the professions but not
normally requiring qualifications of university degree standard;
persons engaged in artistic work and not employing others thereat; and
persons engaged in occupations otherwise included in Group (6) who have
an additional and formal supervisory function.
(6) Junior non-manual workers
Employees, not exercising general planning or supervisory powers,
engaged in clerical, sales and non-manual communications and security
occupations, excluding those who have additional and formal supervisory
functions.
(7) Personal service workers
Employees engaged in service occupations caring for food, drink,
clothing and other personal needs.
(8) Foremen and supervisors - manual
Employees (other than managers) who formally and iirmiediately super-
vise others engaged in manual occupations, whether or not themselves
engaged in such occupations.
(9) Skilled manual workers
Employees engaged in manual occupations which require considerable
and specific skills.
(10)
Semi-skilled manual workers
Bnployees engaged in manual occupations which require slight but
specific skills,
(11) Unskilled manual workers
Other employees engaged in manual occupations.
(12) Own account workers (other than professional)
Self employed persons engaged in any trade, personal service or
manual occupation not normally requiring training of university degree
standard and having no employees other than family workers.
{86963A)
xlii


---

## Page 322

(13) Farmers - employers and managers
Persons who ovm, rent or manage farms, market gardens or forests,
employing people other than family workers in the work of the enterprise.
(14) Fai^mers - own account
Persons who own or rent farms, market gardens or forests and having
no employees other than family workers,
(15) Agricultural ?;orkers
Employees engaged in tending crops, animals, game or forests, or
operating agricultural or forestry machinery,
(16) Members of armed forces.
The detailed allocations of the occupation/employment status groups to
the social classes and socio-economic groups are given in the Classification
of Occupations, I960,
OQNVENTIQN AS TO DOTS (J AND DASHES (-)
In the tables, a dot (. ) indicates that figures are not available or that the
column heading is not relevant to the particular line on which the dot is shown;
a dash (-) indicates that the quantity is zero or, in the case of rates, less than
half of the final digit.
OQMPLETIQN AND REVISION OF CENSUS INFDBMATIQN
Yi/hen collecting the Census returns, the enumerators were required to examine
them for obvious omissions and errors, asking such questions from the persons
delivering the returns as seemed necessary to complete and correct them.
Further
scrutiny and revision of the returns took place at various stages of the work in
the Census Office.
In some cases, notably where ages had been omitted, enquiries
were instituted; otherwise, information was inserted or amended only if reasonable
assumptions could be made from other particulars on the returns.
Prior to compila-
tion, the data extracted from the returns were edited for impossible and unlikely
combinations, corrections being made by reference back to the returns.
Finally,
the tabulations were checked for compatibility of related data.
(86963A)
xiv


---

## Page 323

PART II
STATISTICAL NOTES
The explanatory notes and definitions in Part I apply equally to the notes
which follow and to the tables in Part III.
AREA
Excluding the larger rivers, lakes and tideways, the area of the County at
Census day was 609,030 acres or 952 square miles.
This represents 13 per cent of
the area of Northern Ireland.
In 1896 and 1899 the County lost an aggregate of
almost five square miles to Belfast County Borough and to County Antrim, but gained
in the latter year over half a square mile from County Armagh (see note to Table 2).
POPULATION
The total population of the County was 266,939, which is 25,758 persons or
10.7 per cent more than at the 1951 Census.
Table 2 shows the trends since 1821 when the first complete Census of Ireland
was taken.
Between that year and 1841, the population rose from 325,410 to
361,487 persons which is the highest yet recorded.
Thereafter, there was a decline
which continued with varying intensity until the 1911 Census, by which time the
population had fallen to 204,303, i.e. a decrease of 43 per cent during the seventy
years 1841-1911.
This trend was reversed in the intercensal period 1911-26; and,
while the increases during that and the 1926-37 periods were comparatively small,
substantial population increases of 14.5 and 10.7 per cent in the respective sub-
sequent periods brought the 1961 total to the highest since 1871.
In the Report
for Belfast County Borough, it was pointed out that, a marked decrease in population
in the County Borough was accompanied by a correspondingly greater increase in the
immediate environs of the City.
In this connection, it may be noted that some
78 per cent of the 1961 population increase in County Down as a v^rhole took place
in the portion of the County adjoining the County Borough, viz., Castlereagh and
Hillsborough Rural Districts.
Natural Increases Figures of the natural increase, actual variation and net
inward or outward movement of the population in each intercensal period since 1901
are given in Table I.
Because certain registration districts formerly extended
over parts of both Belfast County Borough and County Down, figures for the County
are not available for periods prior to 1901.

## Page 324

The increase in population since 1937 reflects a relatively much greater
natural increase (i.e* excess of births over deaths) than in the earlier part of
the century and a net movement of persons into the County,
To some extent these
two factors are related, in that much of the. post-war movement into the County
undoubtedly consisted of growing families from Belfast County Borough which settled
in the nevv houses in the vicinity of the City.
As is seen from Table 3, in the

    ten years 1951-61 there was an aggregate net movement of 13,166 persons into
Gastlereagh and Hillsborough Rural Districts, compared with a net inward movement
of 2,851 for the County as a whole.
The urban areas showed a total net inward
movement of 431 persons, the migration in individual areas ranging from + '2,879 in
Bangor ?^unicipal Borough to - 2,464 in Newry Urban District.
In the latter case,
part of the outward movement was probably due to a shift of population to new
houses (some of which were erected by Newry Urban District Council) in the adjoin-
ing Newry No. 2 Rural District- of County Armagh.
All rural districts except
'astlereagh and Hillsborough had a net outward migration, amounting to 10,766
persons for the six districts concerned.

    since 1901 and the variations during the period 1951-61.

/Estimated Xlgures.
Population increases took place in eight of the eleven urban areas.
Bangor
iVunicipal Borough, + 3,236 persons or 15.7 per cent, had the largest actual increase
but Holywood and Newcastle Urban Districts showed'relatively greater increases of
27.7 and 20.9 per cent, respectively.
Decreases occurred in the Urban Districts
of Donaghadee (-5.3 per cent), Wewry (-6.3 per cent) and Dromore (- 11.1 per cent),
the 1961 population of the last named town being the lowest at any Census since
1351.
In rural areas, the most significant feature was the growth of population,
already referred to, in Castlereagh Rural District (+ 16,974 persons or 82.9 per
cent) and to a lesser extent in Hillsborough Rural District (+ 3,166 or 13,2 per
cent).
Except for Banbridge and Kilkeel Rural Districts with decreases of 6*2 and
2.5 per cent, respectively, the remaining administrative rural areas had small
population increases varying between 3.4 per cent in Newry No.l and 0.8 per cent
in North Down.
(86963A)
XVl


---

## Page 325

Of the total population 30.9 per cent were in the municipal boroughs and urban
districts, compared with 31.7 per cent in such areas at the 1951 Census.
It should
be noted, however, that some administrative rural areas contain considerable portions
which are virtually urban in character: for example some 40,000 persons in
Castlereagh and Hillsborough Rural Districts were enumerated in built up localities
extending from Belfast County Borough,
If this is taken into account, it is
estimated that at least 50 per cent of the County population in 1961 lived in urban
communities.
Population densities in the administrative ixrban areas, in terms of persons per

    acre, averaged 8.46 and ranged from 17.71 in Newry Urban District to 2.67 in Kilkeel.
In rural districts, the density averaged 0.31 persons per acre and reached 3.19 in
Castlereagh, the latter being not only considerably higher than in any other rural
district in the County but also higher than in Kilkeel and Newcastle Urban Districts.
Figures of population together with the numbers of habitable buildings and
private households are given in Table 4 in respect of smaller territorial units,
viz., wards of administrative urban areas, district electoral divisions of rural
districts and towns with no legally defined boundaries.
Parliamentary Constituencies; The constituent divisions of the County for the
purpose of elections to the Parliaments of both Northern Ireland and the United
Kingdom are set out in Table 11 together with figures of population, the number of
electors and the ratio of electors per 1,000 population in each constituency.
PRIVATE DWELLINGS AND HOUSEHOLDS

    Total Dwellings: In 1961 there were 76,531 private dwellings of which 71,509
were inhabited and 5,022 were uninhabited.
This represents an increase of 11,432

    dwellings or 17.6 per cent on the corresponding 1951 total of 65,099 (inhabited
60,614; uninhabited 4,485),
The intercensal variations are shown by administra-
tive areas in Table, IV.

*Table I I I , dealing wltii the environs of Belfast, appeared only in the Belfast County Borougn volume,
/As constituted at the date of the 1961 Census,
With the exception of Banbridge Rural District in which there was a decrease of
259 in the total dwellings and Dromore Urban District which showed no change, all
administrative areas shared in the increased number of private dwellings.
In the
urban areas there was an aggregate increase of 4,168 dwellings or 20.3 per cent,
with Bangor Municipal Borough {+ 1,797 awellings or 28.4 per cent), Holywood Urban
District (+ 634 dwellings or 37.9 per cent) and Newtownards Municipal Borough
(86963A)
xvil


---

## Page 326

(+ 632 dwellings or 20.0 per cent) returning the largest individual increases.
Increases of 4,772 dwellings (+ 89.4 per cent) in Castlereagh Rural District and of
1,403 dwellings (+ 23.3 per cent) in Hillsborough Rural District accounted for the
greater part of a net increase of 7,264 or 16.3 per cent in the number of dwellings
in rural areas.
Of the total dwellings, 6.6 per cent were uninhabited in 1961 compared with
6.9 in 1951,
The highest proportions were in those districts v^hich extend to the
coast-line: for example, 13.8 per cent in Donaghadee Urban District, 12.4 per cent
in North Down Rural District, 12.2 per cent in Newcastle Urban District and 9.9 per
cent in Kilkeel Rural District.
In these areas many of the dwellings returned as
uninhabited were, no doubt, casual or summer residences.
It should also be noted
that the total of 5,022 uninhabited dwellings included an unspecified number of
homes from which the usual occupants were only temporarily away on Census night.
According to statistics furnished by the Ministry of Health and Local Govern-
ment, approximately 18,000 new units of dwelling accormnodation were provided in the
County during the intercensal period.
When this figure is compared with the Census
increase of just over 11,400 in the total inhabited and uninhabited dwellings, it
follows that some 6,600 dwellings recorded in 1951 either had ceased to exist by
1961 or were no longer used for private dwelling purposes.
Inhabited Dwellings: Table 6 shows, in respect of the County and each
administrative area, the numbers of inhabited private dwellings, rooms and persons
therein.
Excluding information on type and tenure of accommodation which was
collected for the first time in 1961 and which is dealt with in later paragraphs,
summary figures for the County are given in Table V together with comparable
figures at previous Censuses.

During the period 1951-61 the number of inhabited private dwellings increased
at a proportionately greater rate than the population therein as follows:-

    + 10,895 dwellings or 18.0 per cent; + 26,378 persons or 11.4 per cent.
As the.
average number of rooms per dwelling was also greater than in 1951, there was,
therefore, a reduction in the average niombers of persons per dwelling and per room,
which continued the trend revealed at previous Censuses.
At 3.60 persons per

    dwelling and 0.75 persons per room, the 1961 average densities were 16 per cent and
21 per cent, respectively, below the corresponaing averages in 1926.
Shared
dwellings, i.e. those containing more than one household, accounted for only 0.2
per cent of the total inhabited dwellings compared with 0.8 per cent in 1951 and
0.9 per cent in 1926.
Dwellings with 4-5 rooms increased by 10,718 and now form

    some 58 per cent of the total as against 51 per cent in 1951 and 38 per cent in
1926; those with six or more rooms increased by 3,546 but their proportion was
{86963A)
xvUl


---

## Page 327

still much the same as at the three previous Censuses.
The number of 1-3 roomed
dwellings decreased by 3,369 and their proportion of the total dwellings fell from
S7 per cent to 18 per cent which was less than half of the 1926 ratio.

dwellings by room categories, together with average population densities per

per dwelling and the numbers of persons per dwelling and per room did not differ

Of the urban areas, the coastal towns of Newcastle (5.80), Holywood (5.59) and
Bangor (5.37) had the highest average numbers of rooms per dwelling; Newtownards

    Municipal Borough (4.42) and Downpatrick (4.57) and Newry (4.76) Urban Districts
had the lov^rest averages.
As to the number of persons per dwelling, the urban

    averages ranged from 4.12 in Downpatrick and 4.10 in Newry to 3.10 and 3.07 in
Donaghadee and Bangor, respectively.
In rural areas the averages were less disparate and, in point of accommodation,
were generally most favourable in the three rural disr"tricts nearest to Belfast, viz.
Castlereagh, Hillsborough and North Down.
Type and Tenure of Inhabited Dwellings: Of the 71,177 inhabited permanent
dwellings, 61,376 or 86.2 per cent were classified as dwelling houses.
Of the
latter 43 per cent were owner occupied, 27 per cent were rented from local or
public authorities and 27 per cent were rented unfurnished from other landlords.
Those rented furnished from other landlords accounted for one per cent, the
remainder (between two and three per cent) being occupied in some other way.
At 1,770 the number of flats represented 2.5 per cent of the total inhabited
permanent dwellings.
Some 78 per cent of the flats were rented from local or
(86963A)
xlx


---

## Page 328

public authorities and a further 15 per cent were rented, mostly unfurnished, from
other landlords.
Farmhouses numbered 8.031 or 1L3 per cent of the total dwellings.

houses and flats in each main tenure category, together with the niimber of farm-
houses in each area.

The proportion of owner occupied to total inhabited dwelling houses was
highest in Bangor Municipal Borough (63.2 per cent) and Hillsborough (54,1 per cent)
and Castlereagh (53.8 per cent) Rural Districts; it was lowest in Banbridge
(17.3 per cent) and Dromore (19.8 per cent) Urban Districts.
The areas with the
highest percentages of dwelling houses rented from local or public authorities were
Do^mpatrick Urban District (37.9 per cent) ana Newry Urban District (36.3 per cent).
At the other extreme, only one in eight dwelling houses in Bangor Municipal Borough
and about one in six in Donaghadee Urban District and Hillsborough Rural District
were rented from local or public authorities.
In the case of dwelling houses
rented from other landlords, the proportions varied from 59,4 per cent in Dromore

    Urban District and 52.5 per cent in Newtownards Municipal Borough to 17.7 and 10.4
per cent in Kilkeel and Castlereagh Rural Districts, respectively.
The areas with the most significant numbers of flats were Castlereagh Rural
District (539), Newtownards Municipal Borough (294) and Bangor Municipal Borough
(221), which together accounted for 60 per cent of the total flats in the County.
Over 90 per cent of the flats in Newtowmards Municipal Borough and Castlereagh Rural
District were rented from local or public authorities.
Of the 8,031 farmhouses in
the County, 22 were situated in administrative urban areas; of the latter, 13 were
in Kilkeel Urban District.
In the rural areas other than Castlereagh Rural District
which contained only 82 farmhouses, the proportion of farmhouses to total permanent
dwellings ranged from approximately one in three in Banbridge and Newry No.1 Rural
Districts to less than one in six in Hillsborough ana North Dovjn.
The number of inhabited dwellings returned as non-permanent was 332, of which
324 were stated to be normally occupied all the year round.
Their classification
(86963A)

rented from other landlords - furnished 26, unfurnished 112; occupied in some other
way 20.

Private Households: Table 7 details the numbers of private households in the
County and each administrative area by household sizes, rooms occupied and densities

    of room occupation.' There were 71,703 private households in 1961 or 10,479 more
than at the previous Census.
This represents an increase of 17.1 per cent, which
may be contrasted with increases of 18.0 per cent in the niomber of inhabited private
dwellings, 24.0 per cent in the number of rooms occupied and 11.4 per cent in the
private household population.
The average size of households was 3.59 persons,

    compared with 3.77 in 1951 and 4.23 in 1926.
The nvimher of households in each size group from one to six persons per house-
hold was considerably higher than in 1951, the aggregate increase being 10,689.
Households in the categories from seven to ten-persons decreased by 261 but this was
partly offset by a net increase of 51 in the number of households consisting of 11
or more persons.
The increases v^rere numerically greatest in the tvro-persons
(+ 3,973) and one-person (+ 2,068) households and the latter returned the largest
relative increase.
Intercensal changes in respect of the main size groups were as
follows: -

Expressed as percentages of the total households, the largest groups v^rere:-

    two-persons 23.5; three-persons 20.3; four-persons 18.3 and five-persons 11.9.
In 1951, the three-persons households (21.0 per cent of the total) slightly out-
n-umbered the two-persons households, the latter being followed by four-persons (18.1)
and five-persons (12.4) households.
The percentage for the one-person group

    increased from 9.8 in 1951 to 11.3 in 1961.
The average size of private households in administrative urban areas was 3.41
persons, compared with 3.67 in rural areas and 3.59 for the County as a whole.
In
individual areas the averages ranged from 4.04 in Downpatrick Urban District and
Newry No.1 Rural District to 3.10 in Donaghadee Urban District and 3.06 in Bangor
Municipal Borough.
All areas showed lower average sizes of households than in
1951.
(86963A)

Rooms and Density of Occupation: Intercensal changes in the proportion of
households in each room category are indicated by the following summary:-
Percentage of households occupying

In 1951, households occupying four rooms formed the largest individual group
and accounted for S9.7 per cent of the total households, compared with 20.8 per cent
for households occupying five rooms.
By 1961, however, the niomber of households

    with five rooms, which had increased from 12,718 to 20,817 and from 20.8 to 29.0
per cent of the total households, was slightly greater, than the number of households
occupying four rooms.
At the 1951 Census, households of three persons in four rooms
were in the majority followed by households of two persons in four rooms, whereas in
1961 the latter group had become the most frequent with four-persons households
occupying five rooms in second place.
Table IX shows for each administrative area the 1961 relative distribution of
households oy rooms occupied, together with the percentages of households and
population living at a density of over two persons per room.
(Such a density has
been chosen to provide a convenient comparison with information published at previous
Censuses and not as a measure or definition of overcrowding.
Data for a limited
sub-division of a density of two or less persons per room are given in Table 7;
figures for almost any required density can be arrived at from the details in the
Table).
As households sharing accommodation with other households represented less
than 0.5 per cent of the total households, the distribution of households by rooms
occupied was virtually the same as that of private dwellings by rooms therein.
Such
distribution varied considerably as between one area and another; and it will be
seen that the proportion of households occupying less than four rooms was generally
much higher in rural than in urban areas.
The main exceptions were Downpatrick
Urban District where close on one in four households occupied less than four rooms
and Castlereagh Rural District with about one in twelve, the latter being only
slightly above the corresponding proportions in Bangor Municipal Borough and
Warrenpoint Urban District of 7.5 per cent and 8.1 per cent, respectively, which were
the lowest recorded for any administrative area.
The highest proportion was in
Newry No.l Rural District where 31.6 per cent of the households had less than four
rooms.
(86963A)

^Figures relate to areas as existing at the 1951 Census.
Households in the County accommodated at a density of over two persons per room
numbered 1,683 or 2.4 per cent of the total households, compared with 5.0 per cent
in 1951 and 9.4 per cent in 1925.
The proportion of households at over two persons
per room in each urban area was below the County average except in Downpatrick (5.2,
per cent) and Newry (3.8 per cent); it was lowest at 0,2 per cent in Bangor
Municipal Borough.
Castlereagh (only 0.5 per cent), Hillsborough and North Down
Rural Districts also rettirned proportions below the County average, but in most of
the other administrative rural areas, particularly Newry No.1, the number of house-
holds at over two persons per room was relatively high.
Households at a density of 1^-2 persons inclusive per room accounted for 8.0
per cent of the total households; those at one and over but under 1^ persons per
room represented 24.2 per cent.
Households returning an average density of less
than one person per room formed 65.4 per cent of the total, as against 56.6 per cent
in 1951.
The number of persons residing at over two per room constituted 5.7 per cent of

    the total private household population, compared with 10.3 per cent in 1951 and 16.9
per cent in 1926.
The proportions decreased in all administrative areas and ranged
from 15.6 per cent in Newry No.1 Rural District, 11.6 per cent in Downpatrick Urban
District and 11.5 per cent in Kilkeel Rural District to 1,3 per cent in Castlereagh
Rural District and as low as 0.5 per cent in Bangor Municipal Borough.
On average,
the proportion of private household population living at over two persons per room
in the administrative urban areas was less than half of that in the rural districts.
Persons living at densities of 1^-2 persons and one and over but under 1^
persons per room represented 14.3 per cent and 31.4 per cent, respectively, of the
total private household population.
In the under one person per room
category, the percentage for the County was 48.6; the corresponding percentages for
individual areas ranged from 72.4 in Bangor Municipal Borough and 66.7 in Newcastle
Urban District to 33.3 in Downpatrick Urban and 32.7 in Newry No. 1 Rural Districts.
The number of households with three or more persons living in a single room

    decreased during the last intercensal period from 161 to 58; of the latter, 16 were
in urban areas and 42 in rural areas.
(86963A)
xxiil

## Page 332

Household Arrangements: Table 8 shows, in respect of the arrangements on
which information was soiaght in the Census return, the extent to which private
households had access to such arrangements in their dwellings and whether they had
exclusive use of the arrangements or shared them with other households.
The infor-
mation is analysed according to the number of households per dwelling and the tenure
and type of accommodation occupied.
In the County as a whole, 39,657 households or 55.3 per cent.had sole use of
all six arrangements and a further 68 households or 0.1 per cent shared all arrange-
ments with other households.
Households without any of the arrangements numbered
3,975 or 5.5 per cent of the total households.
Of the households with none of the
arrangements, some 46 per cent were in owner-occupied accommodation and a further
36 per cent rented their accommodation unfurnished from private landlords; over
three-quarters occupied dwelling houses and just under one-fifth occupied farm-
houses.
The arrangements available most were a fixed cooking stove or range
(available to 87.7 per cent of all households), a kitchen sink (82.0 per cent), a
cold water tap (78.1 per cent) and a water closet (74.7 per cent).
Those possessed
by fewest households were a hot water tap and a fixed bath; 28,111 households or

    39.2 per cent had no hot water tap and 29,642 or 41.3 per cent lacked a fixed bath.
Table X shows the numbers of households, expressed as percentages of the total
households, which had use (sole or shared) of the various arrangements according to
the tenure and type of accommodation occupied.
Households in accommodation
provided by local or public authorities had relatively the greatest availability of
arrangements; for example, 69.0 per cent of such households had use of all six
arrangements, compared with 62.2 per cent for households in owner-occupied accommo-
dation and an average of 55.4 per cent for all households.
The proportion of
households with all or any of the arrangements, particularly hot water taps and
fixed baths, was lowest in the case of households in accommodation rented unfurnished
from private landlords.
As regards the availability of arrangements according to
the type of accommodation occupied, households in flats generally fared better than
those in dwelling houses; the latter, in turn, had a much greater availability of
arrangements than households occupying farmhouses.

Table XI compares on a percentage basis the extent to which households had sole
or shared use of the arrangements in the various administrative areas of the County.
The proportion of households with all arrangements varied from 94.5 per cent
in Castlereagh Rural District and 87.5 per cent in Bangor Municipal Borough to
30.3 per cent in Dromore Urban District and 23.6 per cent in Banbridge Rural
District.
Except in Banbridge, Downpatrick and Dromore Urban Districts, at least
nine out of every ten households in urban areas had use of a cold water tap or
water closet or both; a similar proportion was also recorded for Castlereagh Rural
District, but in the remaining rural areas the ratios for households with a water
supply or water closet within their dwellings vjere generally much smaller and less
than one in two in Banbridge Rural District.
As regards hot water taps and fixed
baths, Castlereagh Rural District returned a 95 per cent availability, followed by
(86963A)
XXlV

## Page 333

approximately 90 per cent in Bangor Municipal Borough; at the other extreme, these
arrangements were at the disposal of only 36 per cent of the households in Dromore
Urban District and under 30 per cent of those in Banbridge Rural District,
It may
be mentioned that the proportion of households with the use of a hot water tap or
fixed bath was higher in Castlereagh Rural District than in any administrative area
of Northern Ireland except Whitehead Urban District.

Household Socio-economic Groups: Table 9 analyses, by the socio-economic
group of the head of household, the numbers of private households and persons
therein according to the tenure of the accommodation and the number of rooms
occupied by the household.
Except for that relating to rooms, the information is
summarised in Table XII to provide a convenient comparison between the main groups.
A brief definition of each socio-economic group is given in Part I of the Report.
Approximately 93 per cent of the heads of households who were farmers (S.E.G's

    13 and 14) and 73 per cent of employers and managers (S.E.G's 1 and 2) owned their
accommodation, compared with 25 per cent for semi-skilled manual workers (S.E.G. lO),
23 per cent for personal service workers (S.E.G.7) and 20 per cent for unskilled
manual workers (S.E.G.11) and agricultural workers (S.E.G.15).
Householders in accommodation rented from local or public authorities accounted
for 1.5 per cent of those in the farmers' group, 6.3 per cent of professional
workers (S.E.G^s 3 and 4) and 7.8 per cent of employers and managers.
Excluding
S.E.G. 16, the proportion was highest at an average of some 40 per cent in the case
of manual workers classified to S.E.G's 9-11.
In the category of householders who
rented their accommodation from other landlords, the proportions ranged from 39.6
per cent for personal service workers to 13.2 per cent for professional workers and
slightly over 4 per cent for farmers in S.E.G's 13 and 14.
Households in accommodation occupied in some other way represented only 2.4 per
cent of the total households.
The proportion for each socio-economic group was
also small except in the professional workers' group where it reached 14.4 per cent
and the agricultural workers' group with 9.2 per cent.
A probable reason for the
former is that the professional workers' group includes clergymen, many of whom
occupied rent-free accommodation provided by their church authorities; similarly,
the agricultural workers' group no doubt contains relatively more householders who,
by virtue of their employment, lived in rent-free dwellings than those in the
remaining groups.
(86963A)

Table XII also compares the average sizes of households by socio-economic
groups.
Against an average of 5.59 persons for all households, the highest
averages were 4.20 for households in the unskilled manual workers group and 4.09 for
those in the group consisting of farmers who were employers and managers (S.E.G.13).
The lowest averages were 3.33 persons for households of y\rhich the heads were
intermediate and junior non-manual workers (S.E.G^s 5 and 6) and 2.65 for those in
the personal service workers' group.
Viiith regard to households in the residual
category of "Indefinite and not applicable", it is pointed out that such households
include those of which the heads had no occupation othe!r than that of housewife and
who were accordingly excluded from the socio-economic classification; in many
cases the household heads were either widows or married women whose husbands were
away on Census night.
Calculations based on the data in Table 9 enable a general comparison to be
made of the average sizes of households by tenure of the accommodation occupied as
follows: -
Tenure
Average size of household
Owner occupied
Rented from local or public authorities
Rented from other landlords
Occupied in some other way

4.26

3.28
It will be seen that the average size of households in accommodation rented from
local or public authorities was appreciably greater than that of households in the
other main tenure categories.
USUAL ADDRESS
Table 12 discloses that 98.7 per cent of the total enumerated population had
usual addresses in the County.
Of the remainder, 2,047 persons or 0.8 per cent of
the total normally lived elsewhere in Northern Ireland, and 1,260 persons or 0.5 per
cent were usually resident outside Northern Ireland.

    Sex distribution: In 1961 there were 1,050 females to every 1,000 males
compared with 1,048 in 1951.
Except at age 20 years, males exceeded females at all
ages up to and including S3 years.
At each age from 24 to 41 years, females were
in the majority and continued to predominate at most ages thereafter, the disparity
being relatively more pronounced at the higher ages.
In the combined groups aged 70
and over there were approximately ten women to every seven men.
Ages: Compared with the figures at the previous Census, there were increases
in the number of persons in each quinquennial age group from 0-19 years, particularly

    in the group 10-14 years where males increased by 35.8 per cent and females hy 32,4
per cent.
Altogether the figure for young persons under twenty years of age rose
by 13,489, which was more than half of the intercensal increase in the total
(86965A)
xxvli


---

## Page 336

population of the County.
In the combined group 20-^29 years, there was a decrease
of 2,054 persons (-6.0 per cent) of whom three-quarters were females.
Except for

    relatively small reductions in males aged 30-34 and 70-74 years, increases were
recorded in the numbera of both men and women in all quinquennial groups from
30 years upwards.
One widower and one widow were returned as
iinmarried male at the 1951 Census.

Marital Condition: Although the total persons aged 16 years (the statutory
minimum age of marriage) and over increased by 14,433 or 8.3 per cent, the number

    of single persons of marriageable age was fewer by 4,075 (2,001 males and 2,074
females) or 6.2 per cent.
The niomber of married persons aged 16 and over rose by

    17,584 (8,968 males and 8,616 females) or 19*1 per cent, increases having occurred
in the figures for each sex at almost every individual year of age.
Widowers
decreased by 477 whilst the nxmiber of widows increased by 1,330.
Of divorced

    persons who had not remarried, males increased from 87 to 98 and females from 141
to 201.

BIRTHPLACE AND NATIONALITY
Table 15 shows the population of the County by sex and place of birth.
Of the
total with stated place of birth, 60.4 per cent were born in the County, 20.1 per
cent in Belfast County Borough and a further 9.6 per cent in the rest of Northern
Ireland.
The percentages for persons born in the Irish Republic and Great Britain
(including the Isle of Man and Channel Islands) were 3.4 and 5.7 respectively.
Less than one per cent were retinrned as born outside the British' Isles.
In 1951,
the proportion of the population born in Belfast County Borough was 13.6 per cent;
the increase to 20.1 per cent in 1961 is another pointer to the substantial move-
ment of persons from the City to the County during the intercensal period, already
referred to in this Report.
Changes in the general pattern at each Census since 1861 are outlined in

Among persons born outside Northern Ireland, the proportion of Presbyterians
was much lower and that of members of the Church of Ireland was much higher than
the corresponding proportions which the totals in these denominations bore to the
total population of the County.
As regards the other main groups, the religious
distribution of persons born outside Northern Ireland was not appreciably different
from that of the population as a whole.
Table 17 analyses the number of persons born outside the British Isles by sex
and usual address on 23rd April, 1960, distinguishing between British or Common-
wealth citizens and persons of alien nationality-
Of 2,222 persons with birthplaces
outside the British Isles, 86.3 per cent were British or Commonwealth citizens, 13.0
per cent were aliens and the remaining 0,7 per cent omitted to answer the nationality
question.

xxix


---

## Page 338

In relation to the total population, Roman Catholics accounted for 28.6 per
cent which was the lowest ratio at any Census during the past hundred years.
While
Presbyterians still formed the largest single denomination, their proportion fell

    from 36.4 per cent in 1951 to 35.7 per cent in 1961 in continuance of the downward
trend revealed at each Census since 1861 when the percentage stood at 44.6.

    The percentage for Church of Ireland was 22.9 compared with 83.5 in 1951 and 24.1 in
1926.
At 4.5, the percentage for Methodists showed a small increase on the 1951
ratio; that for the other and not stated denominations rose from 6.1 to 8.3.
How-
ever, with most if not all denominations, comparison with earlier years is slightly
vitiated by the increase from 1,014 to 5,323 in the number of persons who did not
state a religious profession - an increase which probably reflects a much greater
awareness in 1961 that it was not obligatory to answer the Census question on
religion.
Table 19 shows the numbers of males and females in each of the main denomina-
tions by individual years of age to 21 and by quinquennial groups from 20 years
onwards.
Between 1951 and 1961, the number of persons returned as Roman Catholics
under 10 years of age increased by 9.8 per cent while that for all others in the
same age group increased by 8.4 per cent; in the range 10 to 21 years, respective
increases of 13.8 per cent and 25.3 per cent were recorded.
At ages over 21, the
ni;imber of Roman Catholics decreased by 0.3 per cent, compared with an increase of
11.4 per cent for all others.
The percentages of persons by religions in certain age groups to the total
population in those groups, with comparable figures for 1951, are given in Table XVII

While the number of Roman Catholics at all ages averaged ,28. 6 per cent of the
total population, the proportions in the age groups under 25.were above the average
and those in the groups from 25 years onwards were below the average.
Apart from
a minor exception in the case of Methodists in the group aged 50 and over, the
position was reversed in each of the other main religious categories.
Generally,
the Roman Catholic population remains the most youthful but its tendency to have an
increasing proportion of young persons in relation to the other denominations is
less apparent in the County than in Northern Ireland as a whole.
TERMINAL EDUGAHQN AGE
Table 20 shows the distribution of the population aged 15 years and over by
terminal education age.
Figures are given by sexes and by individual years of age
from 15 to 24 and age groups thereafter.
As an indication of the relationship
between education and occupation, the information is also analysed by Occupation
Orders in Table 21.
The figures in the 'TSTot applicable" coliamn of Table 20 relate

    mainly to students (3,208 males and 3,203 females); the balance {28 males and 32
females) represents persons who, through infirmity or for other reasons, never had
any full-time education.
A broad comparison by sex and age groups is afforded by Table X7III which shows
the numbers of males and females at various terminal education ages expressed as
percentages of the respective totals with stated terminal education ages in each age
group.
Except for relatively fewer males having remained full-time at school until
their late teens, no very significant differences are revealed in the general
position as between the sexes.

In comparing the figures for individual age groups, regard should be had to
changes which have taken place in the minimum school leaving age.
Although many
children previously remained at school until 14 years of age, attendance to that
age became compulsory in the County in 1926.
This accounts in some measure for
the relatively substantial decrease in the niimbers of persons who left school under

    14 years of age in the Census age group 45-54 compared with the older age groups.
Similarly, the raising of the school leaving age to 15 years in 1957 affected
persons in the age group 15-19, thus tending to vitiate comparison between that
group and the remaining groups.
It should also be noted that the figures in the

    age groups 15-19 and SO-24 cover only persons whose full-time education had ceased
and thus exclude considerable numbers, particularly in the 15-19 age group, still
in full-time attendance at educational establishments; 25.8 per cent of the boys

    and 28,8 per cent of the girls aged 15-19 were in this excluded category.
Table XIX compares, in respect of selected Occupation Orders, the distribution
of males and females by terminal education ages expressed as percentages of the
respective totals with stated terminal education ages in each Order.
The Orders
shown are those in which the number of males or females, as appropriate, with stated
terminal education ages was 1,000 or more.
In the case of males, almost 88 per cent of labourers (Order XVIII) and more
than three out of every four farmers, foresters, fishermen (l), textile workers (X),
construction workers (XV) and transport and communications workers (XIX) had left
school before 15 years of age.
Among the largely non-manual workers, 70.6 per cent of clerical workers (XXI),
76.2 per cent of administrators and managers (XXIV), 85.7 per cent of armed forces
(XXVI) and 87.8 per cent of professional, technical workers, artists (XXV) continued
their full-time education until 15 years of age or later.
Of those in Orders XXIV

    and XXV, 10.4 and 43.9 per cent, respectively, had terminal education ages of 20
and over.

As regards females, four out of every five textile workers (Order X) and
approximately seven out of every ten clothing workers (XI) and service, sport and
recreation workers (XXIII) had terminal education ages under 15 years.
On the
other hand, 85.6 per cent of clerical workers (XXI) and 88.8 per cent of
professional, technical workers, artists (XXV) had terminal education ages of 15 or
over, with 40.9 per cent of those in Order XX\r having remained full-time at educa-
tional establishments until at least 20 years of age.
OGCUPATIC»IS AND INDUSTRIES
In comparing the economic activity information in this Report with that from
earlier Censuses, it should be borne in mind that the 1961 information was collected
in respect of persons aged 15 years and over, i.e. those who had reached the minim-um
school leaving age, whereas the corresponding limits adopted at the 1951 and 1926
Censuses were 14 and 12 years, respectively.
Questions on occupations and
industries were not asked at the 1937 Census.
Occupations: Table 22 gives figures of males and females aged 15 years and
over, distinguishing between the occupied or economically active population
(including persons temporarily out of work) and the economically inactive.
The
occupied population figures are analysed by the full list of occupation Orders and
groups and by industrial status.
In Table 23, the occupied population is further
analysed by sex, marital condition and age.

    Of the 192,539 persons aged 15 and over in the County, 110,793 or 57.5 per
cent were occupied, compared with a 1951 ratio of 56. 6 per cent for occupied
persons aged 14 and over.

    The percentage for males was 85.4 (1951:- 85.6) and for
    females 32.2 (1951:- 29.8).
(86963A)

Table XX shows the numbers of occupied males and females by broad age groups
together with the proportion of occupied to total in each group.
Almost three--
quarters of the boys and two-thirds of the girls in the group 15-19 years were
occupied.
Thereafter, the proportion for females increased slightly in the age
group 20-24 but declined at each higher age group, whereas that for males increased

    to a peak of over 99 per cent in the group 25-44 years.
At ages 65 and over, more
than one in every four men was occupied as against less than one in twenty women.
Comparable 1951 and 1926 ratios are not available, as information on the occupied
population was analysed by ages only for Belfast County Borough and Northern
Ireland as a whole.
In relation to the total persons (including children under the minimum school
leaving age), the occupied population represented 41.5 per cent compared with 42.4
per cent in 1951.
The proportion for males decreased from 30,7 to 29.4 per cent
and that for females increased from 11.7 to 12.1 per cent.
Among occupied males, 35.9 per cent were single, 62.1 per cent were married and
2.0 per cent were widowed or divorced.
The corresponding percentages for females

    were:- single 63.8, married 30.4 and widowed and divorced 5.8.
Comparable propor-
tions at earlier Censuses are not available on a County basis.
Almost 88 per cent
of the total occupied married women were classified under six occupation Orders,
viz., textile workers (Order X), clothing workers (XI), clerical workers (XXI),
sales workers (XXII), service, sport and recreation workers (XXIII) and professional,
technical workers, artists (XXV).
In these Orders, the proportion of married
women to total females varied from 39.3 per cent for service, sport and recreation
workers to 21.0 per cent for clerical workers.
The following statement sets out the percentage distribution of the occupied
population by industrial status.

Table XXI shows the numbers of males and females in the numerically important
occupation Orders and the proportions per 1,000 of the total occupied persons of
each sex.
Approximately half of the occupied males were to be found in four Orders as
follows:- I (Farmers, foresters, fishermen) 19.8 per cent, VII (Engineering and
allied trades workers n.e.c.) 8.9 per cent, XYIII (Labourers n.e.c.) 11.0 per cent,
and XXII (Sales workers) 9.5 per cent.
In the case of occupied females, some
40 per cent were classified to only two Orders, viz., XXI (Clerical workers) 20.6
per cent and XXIII (Service, sport and recreation workers) 19,5 per cent.
Also,
Orders X (Textile workers), XI (Clothing workers), XXII (Sales workers) and XXV
(Professional, technical workers, artists) each accounted for more than one-tenth
of the total occupied females.

Social Classes and Socio-economic Groups; Table 24 analyses the population
aged 15 years and over by social class, socio-economic group and age-group.
The
social class analysis applies only to occupied and retired persons excluding members
of the armed forces and persons with inadequately described occupations.
In the
socio-economic group section of the Table, the heading "Indefinite" relates to
persons with inadequately described occupations.
The "Not applicable" heading
covers students and persons economically inactive other than institution inmates and
retired persons, those in the two last named categories being classified as
appropriate to the socio-economic groups.
The percentage distribution of the persons allocated to the social classes
was as shown below.
I. Professional, etc. occupations
II. Intermediate occupations
III. Skilled occupations
lY. Partly skilled occupations
Y. Unskilled occupations
Within the framework of the classification, 52.4 per cent of the males were
manual workers, 27.4 per cent were non-manual and 20.2 per cent were agricultural
workers.

    The percentages for females were:- manual 59.4, non-manual 57.9 and
agricultural 2.7.

Note:- Figures are given only for Orders showing proportions of 5 or more per 1,000 working
males or females.
Orders III to XVI inclusive form the broad group of manufacturing industries.
They accounted for 28.7 per cent of the total working population, the proportions
for males and females being 25.3 and 36.6 per cent respectively of the number of
working persons of each sex.
Order XX (Distributive trades) was the largest
individual Order, accounting for 14.6 per cent of the total working persons.
It
was followed by Order I (Agriculture, forestry, fishing) 14.4 per cent and Order X
(Textiles) 10,4 per cent.

(b) Dromara town is in the D.E.D's of crossgar (6) and Dromara (59). The entire town contains 280 persons.
